observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Municipal Accountant (MA) to: 

Compute and consistently provide the correct depreciation for the road network system of which no residual value shall be recognized; 

Prepare the necessary adjusting entry as presented below: 

DR: Depreciation Expense – ₱798,423.80
DR: Prior Period Adjustments – ₱314,127,67
CR: Accumulated Depreciation – ͕₱1,112,551.48

Restate the CY 2023 consolidated financial statements by reflecting the correct depreciation expenses for CYs 2017 to 2023 in all the affected components of the financial statements.


Additionally, we recommended that the LCE direct the: 

General Service Officer (GSO)-Designate to prepare and maintain property cards for each of the PPE accounts on the basis of the AIR and PAR;


MA to prepare and maintain property ledger cards;



GSO-Designate and MA to reconcile their own property records; and



Inventory Committee to conduct annual inventory of PPE.



We further recommended that the LCE to:

Create an Inventory Committee, who will temporarily be relieved of all their regular duties, to complete the physical inventory of PPEs in three months or less, and direct the Inventory Committee to prepare PIP, containing at least the specific assignments/duties of the committee members, the cut-off date and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from the start-up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory; 

Submit the approved PIP to the COA AT at least ten calendar days before the start of the scheduled inventory-taking activities; and

Ensure full implementation of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts to verify the existence, condition and accountability of the PPE.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2023, pp. 50-53 | The MA recomputed the corresponding depreciation and adjusted the same in the books of accounts.


The MA made the necessary adjusting entry.




The MA restated the financial statements and submitted the revised version in September 2024, with the corrected figures duly reflected in the CY 2024 comparative financial statements.





The GSO-Designate is in the process of planning the preparation of individual property cards and has reportedly procured materials and forms needed for the task.
The MA has started working on the preparation of property ledger cards.



GSO-Designate and the MA are currently planning the reconciliation of their respective property records to ensure accuracy and consistency.




GSO-Designate conducted physical inventory of PPE in CY 2024.




As stated earlier, the LGU is yet to create their Inventory Committee.






The submission of the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) to the COA AT is yet to be done.



A physical count of PPE is being conducted as part of preparatory activities for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the GSO and MA to prepare and maintain property records for each supply or commodity to facilitate proper inventory Management system.

We also recommended that the LCE direct the following:

The GSO to:

Spearhead the conduct of physical count of inventories on all supplies in the stockrooms every June 30 and December to verify the existence of recorded balances;


Furnish the MA and Department Office/Heads copies of RPCI to facilitate reconciliation of records; and

Reconcile records with the SLs of the MA to ensure the accuracy and completeness of records maintained.



The MA to:

Prepare the correcting entry to adjust the ending inventory accounts as follows:
General Fund (GF) books
DR: Drugs and Medicines for Distribution - 157,695.22
DR: Agricultural and Marine 	Supplies Expense - 2,995,635.00
DR: Welfare Goods Inventory - 109,097.00
CR: Drugs and Medicines Expenses -157,695.22
CR: Agricultural and Marine Supplies for Distribution - 2,995,635.00
CR: Welfare Goods Expense - 109,097.00

Special Fund Books:
DR: Office Supplies Expense- 327,690.00
CR: Office Supplies Inventory - 327,690.00		
Reconcile and update SLs with the stock cards and RPCI as of June 30 and December 31 to accurately present supplies in stock; and


Maintain the perpetual inventory records comprising of Supplies Ledger Cards for each commodity/stock.


Municipal Health Officer (MHO) and Municipal Agricultural Office (MAO) to regularly furnish the GSO with inventory reports, and coordinate with the GSO and MA for the timely reconciliation of inventory records.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2023, pp. 53-58 | The GSO and MA are in the process of planning the preparation and maintenance of said property records.



The GSO has implemented measures to facilitate the regular conduct of physical inventory of supplies every June and December as part of inventory control and monitoring.

The GSO has taken steps to facilitate reconciliation by the conduct of semestral inventory count per office.

Reconciliation of records is being planned to ensure consistency between GSO and Accounting records.





The MA has taken up the said adjusting entry in the LGU’s books.











The Accounting Office maintains SLs and is coordinating with the concerned offices for the preparation of stock cards and RPCI.

Maintenance of Supplies Ledger Cards is being considered as part of the enhancement of the LGU’s inventory system.

Regular meetings are held between the concerned offices to discuss and facilitate the reconciliation of inventory records.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE direct the following:

Department heads concerned to make representations from the source agencies to seek assistance in the liquidation of the said funds; and



MA to adjust the amount of ₱28,875.00 pertaining to the difference between the recorded balance of the ICFP – LGSEF in the financial statements and the report of liquidation and disbursements.

Additionally, we recommended that the LCE direct:

the MA to reconcile the Cash-in-Bank – Fire Code Fees and Due to NGAs – Fire Code Fees ledger balances and afterwards, take up the following entries to properly recognize the receipt of Share from Fire Code fees in CY 2023:

TF books:

GF books:

the MBO and Bureau of Fire Protection Officer to facilitate the appropriation and utilization, respectively, of the 20% share on fire code fees for the improvement and maintenance of fire stations.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2023, 
pp. 60-64 | Management coordinates with source agencies and requests a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) when funds are downloaded by the NGA to enable monitoring of fund utilization.

The MA recorded the necessary adjusting entry in the books.




The MA recorded the required adjusting entries in both the Trust Fund (TF) and GF books.






A supplemental budget was approved through Appropriation Ordinance No. 5-2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE instruct the MT to:

Stop the practice of withholding business taxes from payments to contractors/suppliers since these two are different and distinct from one another, instead collect business taxes on or before January 20th of the subsequent year; and 

Send demand letters to the business address of the contractors/suppliers prior to the deadline of payment to give them time to comply.



Subsequently, we recommended that the LCE initiate the revision of their tax ordinance, through the Sanggunian, to include provisions for the advance collection of business taxes.



Lastly, we recommended that the LCE direct the MA to adjust the affected accounts in the financial statements with the following correcting journal entry: 

DR: Business Tax	 - ₱885,585.56
CR: Other Deferred Credits	 - ₱885,585.56
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2023,
pp. 64-67 | Management continued to withhold business taxes from payment to contractors/suppliers and recognized them as deferred income.


Management committed to issue demand letters to contractors with outstanding obligations.




Management noted the recommendation and stated that it will be considered during the next update of the Revenue Code.




The MA recorded the required adjusting entry in the books.

observations.recommendation:
We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the GSO Designate to:

Conduct inventory of all insurable properties together with the member of the Inventory Committee; and



Prepare complete lists of insurable properties using PIF to facilitate the processing of insurance with the GSIS.


We also recommended that the LCE direct the MBO to include in the ensuing years' budget preparation the appropriation of sufficient amount for the insurance covering all insurable properties of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2023, pp. 72-74 | An ongoing physical count is being conducted to complete the list of insurable properties for submission to GSIS.

Management also noted that the PIF was prepared to facilitate the insurance process.



The LGU has considered the recommendation and included an appropriation for insurance coverage in the annual budget.

observations.recommendation:
We therefore recommended that LCE direct the GFPS to:

Reinforce the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget with a more prudent gender analysis through establishing the cause/s of identified gender issues and thereafter formulate the PPAs that directly address the same; and



For instances when only a portion of a PPA is aligned with GAD, consider attributing the eligible portion through administering the Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines while the non-eligible portion should be charged under an appropriate fund.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2023,
pp. 75-76 | PPAs were formulated with a stronger focus on women-centered initiatives.




LGU said they have yet to familiarize themselves with the use of gender analysis tools.

AAR 2023, pp. 77-79 | Management monitored the utilization of the fund.






Management confirmed that there were no remaining PPAs under Continuing Appropriations as of year-end.




Management planned the utilization of the unexpended balances and facilitated the preparation of a Supplemental AIP for the charging of priority projects under the STF.




Management ensured that no administrative costs were charged against the LDRRMF, consistent with applicable guidelines.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following:
 
MA to ensure that all total costs incurred for all projects, programs and activities are accrued accordingly and that obligations are completely and adequately recognized in the SCBAA; and



MBO to update the Statement of Appropriations and Obligations and ensure that all obligations for the year are reflected therein.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2022, pp. 61-62 | The MA regularly referred to the SAAOB prepared by the MBO in recording obligations.




The Budget Officer was instructed to update the SAAOB to reflect every obligation incurred from available funds.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity (HoPE) direct the Bids and Awards Committee to remind prospective bidders during the pre-bid conference of the completeness of information in the required Class "A" documents, particularly the completeness of declared ongoing and awarded but not yet started government and private contracts and the accuracy of declared NFCC.  Otherwise, disqualify non-compliant bidders during bid evaluation or post qualification based on the grounds set under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2022, pp. 64-66 | HoPE directed the BAC to remind prospective bidders during the pre-bid conference of the completeness of information in the required Class "A" documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Disposal Committee to expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties to free up storage spaces, prevent further deterioration of PPE and generate additional income from sale.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2021, pp. 72-74 | Unserviceable and disposable properties are currently stored in preparation for disposal and possible availment of one-time cleansing.

AAR 2021, pp. 77-79 | Management continues to evaluate transactions to minimize reliance on reimbursement as a mode of disbursement.




Management reviews payment practices to ensure alignment with disbursement protocols.




Management takes steps to strengthen compliance with procurement rules under RA No. 9184, particularly on direct payments to suppliers.




Management reviews the transaction and takes appropriate actions to address the audit recommendation.




Management strengthens internal control practices and ensures adherence to applicable financial regulations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MT to:

Enforce collection of the underdeclared sales noted with corresponding interest, surcharges, and penalties; and



Conduct regular inspection and verification of financial records of the business establishments to ensure that the correct gross receipts will be declared by the taxpayer.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2021, pp. 82-86 | Management is currently assessing appropriate actions to address underdeclared sales and improve revenue enforcement mechanisms.

Initial steps are being considered to institutionalize inspection protocols for validating declared financial records of businesses.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MA to improve internal control process of settlement of AP and to furnish report as a result hereto which may include provision of additional manpower, among others.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2020, pp. 59-64 | The MA has commenced the review of pending accounts payable and has initiated steps to improve monitoring procedures.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MA to:

Complete the data requirements in the Journals and Journal Entry Vouchers and to certify correct them; and


Use the General Journal and the Cash Receipts Journal for the purpose these were created.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020, pp. 81-86 | The MA regularly prepares and updates the data requirements in the journals and Journal Entry Vouchers.

The General Journal and Cash Receipts Journal are already in use and are part of the regular recording process.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Allocate fund intended for the survey/relocation and titling of these lots; and
b. Exert effort in the processing of Certificate of Title of the untitled lots to establish absolute ownership and protect the legal interest of the LGU pursuant to Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386.
observations.managementAction: Request for Supplemental Budget. Exert effort in the processing of Certificate of Title of the untitled lots to establish absolute ownership and protect the legal interest of the LGU pursuant to Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386.
observations.reason: No allocation for surveying and titling of funds. As of yearend, the lots owned by the Municipality remained not titled in the name of the LGU.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management implement the following course of actions:
a. The Municipal Engineer and Chairman of the Inventory Committee to provide the classifications of the projects recorded in the Road Network account; b. The Chairman of the Inventory Committee, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, prepare and maintain an Inventory of all Local Roads and Road Map. Likewise, the preparation of Inventory of Local Roads and Road Map (RLRN and RPCLRN) must be observed yearly; and c. The Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, identify and reconcile the components of each completed projects and compute the accurate depreciation of each road network component and make necessary adjusting journal entries to recognize any differences in the reconciliation of accounting records and the required inventory reports.
observations.managementAction: The MPDO and MEO shall provide the detailed classifications of road such as provincial, municipal and barangay roads within the municipality. The Chairman of the Inventory Committee to conduct updating of Inventory of Local Roads and the Inventory Committee shall conduct yearly. The Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, identify and reconcile components of each completed project and compute the accurate depreciation of the component.
observations.reason: No RLRN and RPCLRN was prepared and submitted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Revise the Economic Enterprise Code to expand penal clause particularly on the operations of the Municipal Economic Enterprise to ensure that the code was efficiently implemented and to expand its penal clause incorporating further legal actions to delinquent consumers whose balances were cut-off but with previously unsettled balances.
observations.managementAction: Comply with the revisiting of the ordinances relative to revenue generations and expand penal clause. Revise the municipal code to expand the penal clause.
observations.reason: No legislation was enacted for the expansion of the penal clause.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management allocate at least ₱35,700.00 and transfer it to the trust fund account exclusively for the operation and maintenance of the completed PRDP subproject; and implement the maintenance works stipulated in the Operation and Maintenance (O&M) Plan.
observations.managementAction: Allocate for the operation and maintenance of the completed PRDP subproject; Implement the maintenance works stipulated in the Operation and Maintenance (O&M) Plan.
observations.reason: Allocation for the O&M was not implemented. The O&M was not transferred to the Trust Fund account. The O&M was not utilized optimally.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management institutionalize the creation of PDAO by taking the following course of actions:
a. Direct the Human Resource Management Office (HRMO) to take the necessary proceedings to create permanent plantilla of positions for the office and exert effort in hiring qualified members of PWDs to fill-in the created positions; and b. Direct the Municipality to allocate PS, MOOE, and CO, if necessary, for the operation of the Office.
observations.managementAction: Direct the HRMO to take necessary proceedings to create permanent plantilla of positions for the office and exert effort in hiring qualified members of PWDs to fill-in the created positions; Allocate PS, MOOE and CO for the operation of the office.
observations.reason: Creation of the Office is still subject for organizational committee hearing. SB ordinance for creation of the office is not yet approved.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management attend to intensify tax campaign and employ collection strategies, and enforce the remedies provided for in the provisions of law to persuade the taxpayers to pay their taxes on time to realize the revenue needed to finance other developmental projects.
observations.managementAction: To intensify tax campaign and employ collection strategies, and enforce the remedies provided in the law; Conducted discussion with LFC and Revenue Heads; Formulation of IRR of Revenue Code and issuance of Executive Order for the Task Force to exert efforts in generating revenues to meet the targets.
observations.reason: The collection efficiency rate remains low.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Inventory Committee to conduct comprehensive physical count of all types of inventories to give enough time to prepare the RPCI and submit the same on or before January 31 of the ensuing year as prescribed in Section 124 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: Directed the Inventory Committee to conduct comprehensive physical count of all types of inventories and prepare and submit RPCI.
observations.reason: No Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) submitted as of December 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the MA to:
Reconcile the Accounting Office’s records with those of the GSO regarding properties with an individual acquisition cost below ₱50,000.00 that are still recorded under the OSMI account and adjust the records accordingly to reflect the issuance of these properties, by taking up the following entry:
Prepare the adjusting entry for the net book value of the properties below ₱50,000.00 booked under the OSMI account, by:
Reconcile with the GSO and prepare necessary adjusting entry to record the issuance of semi-expendable properties recorded under the OSMI account in CY 2024, as follows:

We recommended that the LCE direct the
Require MA and the MBO to establish a formal and regular reconciliation process of budgetary records, particularly before the preparation and submission of year-end financial reports to ensure consistency and accuracy in the figures reflected in the SCBAA; and
Strengthen MA to thoroughly review all budget entries and classifications, to accurately reflect in the Original Budget column of the current year’s SCBAA the Current and Continuing Appropriations ending balances of previous year’s Capital Outlay, in accordance with IPSAS 24.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
ME to conduct a mandatory year-end inspection of all ongoing projects and prepare duly certified inspection reports to support the reported percentage of completion in the Status Report on Quarterly PPAs, to serve as basis for the MA to recognize the corresponding accruals in the books;
MBO to update the SAAOB and ensure that all obligations for the year are reflected therein; and
MA to ensure that the total costs incurred for all PPAs are properly accrued and that obligations are fully and accurately recognized in the SCBAA.

We recommended that the LCE require the:
a.	MA to strictly observe the proper recognition of various assets, AP and expenses by recording liabilities only upon actual delivery of goods or rendering of services, pursuant to the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume III; and
b.	Designated GSO to provide the list of delivered items as ready reference, to facilitate the accurate recording and recognition of assets, as well as the accrual of expenses, at the end of each reporting period.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
MT to:
Direct all Revenue Collection Clerks and/or Authorized Collecting Officers to remit their collections intact daily;
Ensure that municipal collections are deposited intact daily with the Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB) in compliance with the aforecited provisions; and
Immediately discontinue the practice of encashing checks and paying transactions out of the collections.
MA, who serves as the Internal Auditor of the Agency, ensure that the aforementioned regulations are strictly complied with.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the MT to issue OR immediately upon actual receipt of collections, in accordance with the aforementioned regulations.  This is to mitigate the risk of fund misappropriation or loss and to ensure accurate and timely recognition of collections.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the MT to:
Immediately discontinue the practice of utilizing the PCF for the payment of AICS and other recurring operating expenses; and
Strictly utilize the PCF only for authorized, non-recurring, petty operating or miscellaneous expenses that cannot be conveniently paid through check.
We also recommended that the LCE designate a Special Disbursing Officer who shall be responsible for drawing cash advances for the payment of AICS and for ensuring the timely and proper liquidation of such advances.  This approach will help ensure compliance with existing rules and regulations on the proper use of the PCF.

We recommended that the LCE strengthen the Municipality’s internal control system in the evaluation and approval of loans to ensure compliance on the NTHP, by directing the:
AAO/HRMO to:
Strictly comply the provisions of Section 56 of RA 11975, by closely monitoring payroll deductions;
Coordinate with the Municipal Information Technology (MIT) Office for the creation of a centralized database to record and monitor employees’ outstanding loans, which will serve as a reliable reference in the evaluation and approval of future loan applications; and
Conduct extensive information and awareness campaigns for all employees to ensure a comprehensive understanding of loan-related policies and requirements in order to empower employees with essential knowledge, and promote financial literacy within the organization.
MA and MT to refrain from issuing or signing certifications for loan applications in cases where an employee’s NTHP would fall below the minimum threshold of ₱5,000.00, exclusive of other loan obligations.

We therefore recommended that the LCE, being the Chairman of the LSB, to:
Ensure strict compliance with the prescribed timeline under JC No. 1, s. 2017 by convening the LSB earlier in the year to facilitate timely review and approval of the SEF budget, thereby preventing delays in the implementation of SEF-funded programs and activities;
Coordinate with the DepEd representative and relevant municipal offices to monitor the timely submission and approval of planning documents such as the SIDP, and align the SEF budget preparation accordingly; and
Direct the MBO and the MA to ensure that no disbursement is made for SEF-funded activities unless supported by an approved appropriation.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the LSB to ensure that the SEF Budget be presented by P/A/P, by allotment class and per school, using SEF Budget Preparation Form No. 1 (Annex A) in the ensuing budget years, and see to it that lump-sum appropriations and appropriations with generic descriptions be avoided to promote transparency, accountability, and equitable resource allocation among schools.

We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the LSB, in coordination with the MA, to:
Submit the lacking inspection reports, completion certificates, and accomplishment reports to substantiate the validity and propriety of the affected disbursements;
In the ensuing years, ensure that all transactions funded under the SEF are supported by complete and accurate documentation, in compliance with the relevant provisions of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001; and
Seek the assistance of the MA in identifying and complying with the basic documentary requirements necessary for the proper processing and recording of financial transactions.

We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the LSB, in coordination with the MA, to ensure the proper and timely transmittal of the SEF Utilization Report to the DepEd CO, SB, and the ROs of the DBM and DILG as prescribed in DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017.

We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the MENRO to:
Strengthen fund utilization planning by identifying compliance-driven priorities early in the budget cycle and aligning them with available resources, to promote proactive, safe, and standards-based implementation of the SWMP;
Strategically incorporate the procurement and distribution of PPEs into the annual Solid Waste Management Fund programming, ensuring that all collectors and personnel directly involved in the handling of solid waste are adequately equipped in accordance with Section 23 (a) of RA No. 9003 and its IRR; and
Coordinate with the Municipal Health Officer (MHO) or other appropriate agencies to facilitate the conduct of training programs aimed at enhancing the safety awareness and technical competencies of garbage collectors and utility workers. These may include, but are not limited to, TESDA-accredited courses such as Garbage Collector NC I, in compliance with Section 23 (b) of RA No. 9003.

We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal DRRM Officer to:
Strategically schedule and monitor the implementation of PPAs under Disaster Prevention and Mitigation and Disaster Preparedness to ensure that these are conducted well ahead of high-risk periods in line with the proactive intent of JMC No. 2013-1; and
Ensure that planned PPAs, particularly those involving procurement of essential goods and equipment, are implemented on time to maximize their impact on community safety and disaster readiness.

We recommended that the LCE direct the MA and the MBO to discontinue the practice of charging prior year expenditures to current year appropriations, in order to ensure the proper matching of expenses with their corresponding budget year, in accordance with applicable accounting standards and the provisions of Section 305(a) of R.A. No. 7160 and relevant government accounting regulations.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
a.	BAC to determine and utilize the most efficient mode of procurement for POL products;
b.	Department Heads to remind official drivers to prepare the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Report of Fuel Consumption; and
MA to check the completeness of supporting documents of disbursements.

We recommended that the LCE cause the:
Submission of the legal basis supporting the payment of the medical cash allowance; or
Refund of the allowance received, in the absence of a valid legal basis authorizing its payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Exert diligent effort in locating the supporting documents, and if necessary,
obtain from the bank the documents required as bases in recording in the books
the reconciling items of CY 2024 and prior years; and
b. Conduct the immediate reconciliation of the balances of the Cash in Bank-Local
Currency, Current Account under the General Fund with the respective
cashbook/s at least quarterly and make the necessary adjustment/s on the
difference noted in the amount of P14,916,336.64.

We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct
the Inventory Committee, the Municipal Accountant and General Services Office
(GSO) to conduct the one-time cleansing of accounts following the guidelines and
procedures under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and submit to
the Audit Team the proposed timeline for the immediate completion thereof.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant
and GSO to expedite the preparation of PPE ledger cards and property cards,
respectively.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant
to ensure that all reported items in the lapsing schedule have complete details as to
specific descriptions, reference, location and mode of acquisition.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to record the transfer of the completed project
with a contract cost of P8,910,000.00 and carrying value of P8,310,431.25; and
b. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to ensure necessary coordination with the DPWH
on the implementation of government projects, particularly those that are
intended to be subsequently transferred to the LGU, to ascertain timely and
complete recording of assets in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all Department Heads to ensure
that their submitted annual investment plan contains specific work programs so as to
properly account expenditure items.
Moreso, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant
to review the nature of each disbursement and identify the specific expense accounts
to which they should be attributed in order to accurately and fairly reflect the
balances of the financial statements and ensure compliance with COA regulations.

Henceforth, we strongly reiterated that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to strictly implement monthly reconciliation of reciprocal accounts such as
Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds to ensure the accuracy and
reliability of the reported balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records of PPE
and ensure that the noted discrepancy of P167,783,445.45 between the RPCPPE
and the PPE balances presented in the Financial Statements (FS) is addressed
to arrive at the correct and reconciled balance of PPE;

b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to generate and provide supporting
documents such as the "Unreconciled SL", “Reconciling SL”, or the like, to
substantiate the supposed unreconciled beginning balances, if applicable;

c. Direct the Municipal Engineer to extend technical assistance to the GSO and
Municipal Accountant, particularly in the validation and reconciliation of Land
Improvements, Infrastructure Assets, Buildings, and Other Structures; and

d. Direct the Inventory Committee to ensure that the RPCPPE includes all the
necessary information and shall include only PPE which are physically counted,
and to conduct other procedures necessary, if warranted, to complete the one-
time cleansing of PPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit, on or before the 31st day of December
every year, the CLRPTDC for the succeeding year to the Municipal Accountant to
facilitate the recording of the actual real property tax receivables for the year in
the books of accounts;

b. Municipal Treasurer to undertake the necessary actions to update the Real
Property Tax Account Register to facilitate the efficient and effective monitoring
of the real property tax delinquencies in the Municipality and the timely
generation of the Certified List of all Real Property Tax Delinquencies; and

c. Municipal Accountant to set-up the RPT Receivable and SET Receivable at the
beginning of every year based on the CLRPTDC and reconcile any discrepancy
with the Certified List of all Real Property Tax Delinquencies to ensure the
accuracy of the said receivable accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Exert diligent efforts to recover the outstanding accountabilities from the
separated employees and, where necessary, initiate appropriate legal measures
to enforce settlement; and

b. Demand the settlement of all other unliquidated cash advances and strictly
enforce the timely liquidation of all cash advances by ensuring that all cash
advances are fully liquidated at year-end, and any unexpended balance shall be
refunded by the Accountable Officer (AO), except for the petty cash fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Engineer to furnish the Municipal Accountant with status reports,
together with the SWAs, for all completed and on-going projects, with or
without filed progress payments, to ascertain the status and proper recording
in the books of the Municipality; and

b. Municipal Accountant to pass the correcting JEV to reflect the correct balances
of the affected accounts by debiting CIP and PPE accounts by P2,596,356.00
and P9,891,005.04, respectively; and crediting the AP account by
P12,487,361.04, thereafter furnish the Audit Team a copy of the JEV. Moreover,
to ensure that the corresponding depreciation expenses for the recognized PPE
accounts are also provided and recorded accordingly.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to
prepare a complete Schedule of Unreleased Checks at the end of the year and
submit the same to the Municipal Accountant who, on the basis thereof, shall
prepare the JEV for the restoration of cash equivalent of the unreleased checks and
recognition of the appropriate payable/liability accounts at the end of the year, and
in addition, reverse the entries made in the ensuing year to fairly present the
balances of the affected accounts in the SFPos.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to draw entries in the books of the Municipality
to recognize the unrecorded receivable and income in the Rosario Public Market
and Fish Port; and

b. Direct the Supervisors of the Municipal Public Market, Fish Port, and Water
System to furnish the Municipal Accountant with a monthly schedule of
Accounts Receivable to facilitate the timely recording in the books of the
Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the
Provincial Government to determine the origin and details of the reported
discrepancies between the reciprocal accounts Due from LGUs and Due to
LGUs, and make necessary adjustments to ensure that the balances of both
reciprocal accounts are reconciled; and

b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to furnish the Provincial Accountant with
copies of the weekly summary of the JEVs showing the breakdown of amounts
collected by the Municipality due to the Provincial Government, as well as a copy
of the monthly Abstract of the RPT to facilitate reconciliation with the weekly
summary of JEVs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Services Officer and the
Municipal Accountant to prepare the duly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report
of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP), including all other government properties subject for
disposal, accompanied by relevant documents pertaining to the unserviceable properties,
as the case may be, and submit the same to the Disposal Committee for inspection,
appraisal and immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties to prevent further
deterioration, loss and reduction of economic value.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Direct the Local Finance Committee to analyze trends in tax revenue collections
from prior years, determine the amount of income with reasonable assurance
of collectability, and use these data as basis or benchmark in preparing the
Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year;

b. Require Municipal Treasurer to intensify the conduct tax campaign initiatives
to boost the collection and promote prompt payment of taxes due to the
Municipality;

c. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare the Daily Cash Position Report and
monitor the availability of cash to prioritize expenditures to avoid the
incurrence of overdraft; and

d. Direct the Local Finance Committee to adopt measures to eliminate the cash
overdraft by exercising diligence, prudence, and proper control in the Annual
Budget and disposition of the Municipality’s funds, limit expenditures within the
total income collected and realized during the year, and reduce unnecessary
operating expenditures until the cash overdraft is eliminated.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from processing and granting
additional cash advance/s to AOs unless their previous cash advances have
been fully accounted for or liquidated as provided in Section 89 of PD No. 1445
and Item No. 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002, and issue the required
certification before granting any cash advance in compliance with Item no. 1.1
of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and

b. Require every AO who will receive any cash advance to strictly observe the
prescribed liquidation period of his/her cash advance consistent with the
provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002, and Section 109(A)(1) of LTOM 2nd
Edition, 2019, Book III.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant,
Human Resource Management Officer and other concerned officials to ensure that:

a. The ToR of individual consultants whose works are either highly technical or
proprietary or primarily confidential or policy determining, where trust and
confidence are the primary consideration, and the justification for their hiring
are prepared and submitted by the end-user units before procurement or
negotiation;

b. Validation of consultants legal, technical and financial capability and pertinent
recommendation of award have been made by the BAC, pursuant to the 2016
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and

c. Submit justification on the necessity of hiring consultants and lacking
documents, and ensure that the Accomplishment Reports submitted are
supported by detailed information and tangible outputs/reports.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to
ensure that all DVs and their supporting documents are duly stamped “PAID” after
payment is made to prevent the possible re-use of the same on similar future
transactions in line with COA Circular No. 92-389 dated November 3, 1992.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Market Supervisor,
Fish Port Supervisor, and Municipal Legal Officer to facilitate the execution of
contracts between the Municipality and existing Rosario Public Market and Fish
Port stallholders, as implied by the pertinent provisions of the Tax Code of the
Municipality of Rosario.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Local Finance Committee to propose fiscal policies and measures aimed at
improving the financial performance of the Municipality; and

b. Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the
expenditures, ensuring they do not exceed the total income realized during the
year to avoid incurrence of net loss.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor :

a. Direct all concern Municipality Officials and Employees to stop the procurement
and/or payments for goods and services through reimbursements, except those
allowable or emergency needs, and adhere strictly to Section 93 of PD No. 1445
which requires government agencies to make direct payments to suppliers,
contractors and other payees are made in checks to ensure transparency and
accountability of government transactions; and

b. Ensure that taxes on government money payments are properly withheld and
remitted to the BIR and charge to the personal account of the employee
responsible for the withholding and remittances of the tax the interest, penalties
and surcharges that may be incurred for non-compliance with the above-mentioned
BIR regulations.

1.4 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory
Committee to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report on
Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) and to dispose all unserviceable properties at the earliest possible time
pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445 and Sections 379-381 of RA 7160.
Additionally, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor implement the One-
Time Cleansing guidelines prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated
January 31, 2020 and require the Inventory Committee to plan ahead the conduct
of Physical Inventory to be used in the One-Time Cleansing and ensure that the
RPCPPE will be submitted on time.

Furthermore, implement a regular assessment of the impairment of the
LGU’s assets in line with IPSAS.

2.14 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive review the contract
management process to identify the gaps in monitoring, enforcement and
adherence to execution timeline. Further, immediately enforce the
termination of the contract for the Fish Coral and Fishpond project.

Further. We recommended the following corrective measures for the
succeeding infrastructures projects of the LGU:
a. Require the MPDC, Municipal Engineer, and LPMC to coordinate and
intensify the monitoring of all ongoing and completed projects to ensure that
the progress of construction is within the timetable and scope provided in the
agreed construction works.
b. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to immediately inspect any ongoing
construction that approximately reached 95% work accomplishment, and to
prepare the punch list for correction/completion and require the contractor to
perform the satisfactory completion of the project.
c. Otherwise, enforce the sanctions and penalties upon the defaulting
contractor, such as imposition of administrative suspension for one to two
years from participating in all government bidding process, termination of the
contract and forfeiture of the performance security and enforcement of the
liquidated damages, as provided for in Republic Act 9184.

3.16 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive evaluate the
procurement strategies to prevent recurrence of similar issues and ensure
better project execution by employing the following suggested measures:
a. Require the BAC to ensure that no contractor is awarded of multiple
projects with overlapping timeless unless they have proven technical
capacity to execute all projects concurrently without compromising the
quality and timelines of the contract execution.
b. Require the LPMC and the Engineer to establish a regular monitoring
process and progress reporting to ensure that contractors are adhering
to the projects’ construction timeline.
c. Enforce the penalties prescribed in RA 9184 to ensure that all
contractors effectively manage their resources and avoid delays in
project completion.

4.8 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Engineer to post billboards in the project site on all implemented
infrastructure projects and/or to subsequently update the tarpaulin
signboards in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-
004.

Further, require the Accountant to ensure that the pictures of the posted
signboards be part of the supporting documents in the payment of progress
billings of infrastructure projects. Also, appropriately disclose complete
information on the status of the Construction-In-Progress account.

5.10 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MPDC and
other officials concerned to:
a. Conduct early procurement planning, improve market studies, and
ensure compliance with RA 9184 to prevent delays in procurement and
implementation;
b. Require periodic progress reports on fund utilization and project
implementation to facilitate proactive issue resolution; and
c. Otherwise, identify alternative priority projects that can be
implemented within the fiscal year to optimize utilization of the fund.

6.11 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive employ corrective
measures in the budgeting, utilization and reporting LDRMM Fund, such
as:
a. Require the LDRRMC to prepare the LDRRM Plan and Budget that
identify the specific activity or program to be implemented in each
thematic area except for the 30% QRF which shall be presented in
lump sum.
b. Require the Budget Officer to maintain a complete and updated record
on the uses of the LDRRMF and ensure that only those
activity/program that are specifically provided in the LDRRMF Plan
and Budget shall be charged against the fund.
c. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all disbursements are
supported with complete documentation which should include the
specific activity to which the fund was used.
d. Require the LDRRMO to observe the timely submission of the
LDRRMF Utilization Report prepared and certified by the
Accountant.

7.14 To address the inefficient utilization of the GAD fund and ensure that
gender-sensitive activities/programs are incorporated into LGU funded
projects, we recommend the following:
a. Formulate a gender-responsive budgeting framework to ensure that
gender considerations are incorporated at LGU implemented projects.
b. Establish coordination between the GAD Focal person/Office and
other departments concerned to ensure GAD integration into LGU
funded PPAs.
c. Implement a system for tracking and evaluating the GAD Fund
utilization.

8.13 We recommended to the LCE the following measures to address the delay
and staggered payments of Terminal Leave Benefits:
a. Require the HRMO to identify the retired Officers and Employees with
pending claims from the LGU and submit the same together with the
computation of the retirement benefits/pay and propose for a
supplemental appropriation in CY 2025 or for inclusion in the CY 2026
appropriation.
b. Henceforth, require the HRMO to send communication to the retired and
retiring employees to submit their letter of intent and/or provide them
of retirement application form and the list of the documents required.
c. Further, require the MPDC and HRMO to work closely in ensuring
that sufficient funds for employee benefits, most importantly the TLB,
are allocated in the annual budget in each year. This may include
determining the number of employees eligible for retirement or
separation and calculating the amount needed for their terminal
benefits during the budget period.

9.14 We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
a. Require coordination between the MSWDO, MHO and Municipal
Treasurer in determining the available resources of the LGU to ensure
that the recommended assistance are readily available.
b. Coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan and the Municipal Social
Welfare and Development Office in formulating the implementing
guidelines in managing the AICS Program aligned with the DSWD
guidelines in the financial and/or in-kind aid granted to various
individuals in crisis situation.
c. Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to
ensure that all disbursements are covered with a valid appropriation.

10.21 To address these concerns and ensure that hiring practices adhere to the
principles of transparency, efficiency, and necessity, we recommend that
the LCE limit the hiring of Job Order workers to indispensable functions only
and pay the daily minimum wage prescribed by the DBM-CSC-COA
JC No. 1, series of 2017 by employing the following measures:
a. Require the HRMO to determine the necessity, and assess the duties
and functions of JO workers, taking into account their expertise,
experience, and capability to handle the position to come up with the
ideal number of personnel to be hired;
b. Require the MPDC and HRMO to align JO hiring with actual
workload demands, ensuring that personnel are engaged only where
there is a legitimate need. Also, to develop a clear and equitable wage
structure for JO personnel to prevent inconsistencies and unjust
compensation practices.
c. Require all departments to submit detailed justifications for JO hiring,
including workload analysis and impact assessments.d. Provide in the contract of service the specific details or information
such as expected output/service to be rendered, duration of work,
office/project assignment, source of funds, and its daily rate; and
e. Require each JO worker to prepare the accomplishment report, stating
therein the actual and specific tasks undertaken during the period, duly
verified and accepted by the immediate supervisor.

11.13 To address the issues identified we recommend the following courses of
action to the Local chief Executive:
a. Before entering into contract require the finance committee,
particularly the Accountant, Budget Officer and Treasurer, to certify
the availability of funds and appropriately reserve such appropriation
for the payment of service to ensure availability of sufficient fund and
valid appropriation throughout the contract duration.
b. Require the Budget Officer and the Accountant to employ stringent
budget monitoring to ensure that appropriations match the services
rendered and/or contracted.
c. Require the HRMO and the Treasurer to ensure the timely processing
and payment of compensation to JO Employees.

12.9 We recommended that the provision for the HDMF contribution share of
the LGU be appropriately provided in the appropriation of the LGU Funds
for the JO Employees mandated to be enrolled in the HDMF Membership.
Henceforth, ensure that the LGU shall withhold and remit the required
contribution, including the LGU share, to the HDMF.
Henceforth, we recommended that the LGU request HDMF to conduct an
orientation session for the HR and/or Payroll Employees to ensure full
understanding and compliance with HDMF membership requirements
and information dissemination with covered employees.

13.7 We recommended that the LCE take immediate measures to ensure
procurement of comprehensive insurance coverage for all insurable
properties of the LGU, including the vehicles, infrastructure and other
valuable properties. The insurance coverage should be for fire, theft,
natural disasters and other relevant coverage based on the specific risks
faced by the LGU.

Likewise, we recommended that the LCE require the Inventory Committee
to review the existing Inventory List of PPE and identify those that are
insurable and would require insurance coverage. Ensure that the required
PIF is appropriately filled out, updated and accurate, and be submitted to
COA and GSIS to facilitate proper insurance documentation.

14.10 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive exercise due diligence in
securing the LGU funds by employing appropriate steps and procedures
to avoid lapses and late payments of utility bills, such as but not limited to:
a. Designate an office/officer to be responsible in the consolidation of the
bill, preparation of the DV and ensuring that the bills are paid on time,
b. Require the official/office receiving the bill to immediately transmit the
bill to the General Service Office/the designated person responsible in
the preparation of the DV for the payment of the bills.
c. Require the responsible office/officer to prioritize the processing and
payment of the utility bills.

14.11 We recommended for the LCE the following corrective measures to
comply with the statutory and mandatory requirements of HDMF:
a. Designate a specific department/employee to prepare the remittance
documentation and maintain records to ensure that all withheld
contributions are remitted on time.
b. Require the Accounting Department to update the payroll records and
reconcile all withheld loan amortizations and contributions, remit the
amounts immediately, and/or require the employee concerned to
refund the amount remitted in excess of the amount withheld to avoid
further penalties and interest.
c. Designate a temporary Liason to address and resolve any discrepancies
in contributions and remittances with the lending and statutory
institutions.
Likewise, we recommended that remittances of amount withheld shall only
be made after the payment of compensation was made and not upon the
withdrawal of the cash advance. The remittance shall be supported with the
documents or proof of withholding or payment from the employee
concerned.

16.8 We recommended that the Local School Board be actively involved in the
SEF budget preparation process by ensuring that the budget preparation
timelines are sufficiently communicated with all the members of the LSB
to provide them ample time to review and provide inputs on the proposed
budget.
Furthermore, facilitate regular consultations and forums from key
stakeholders, particularly the teachers, parents, and students to encourage
inputs in the identification of the programmed activities/projects to ensure
that the SEF budget aligns with the educational needs of the District.

17.11 To ensure proper utilization and compliance with SEF budgetary
guidelines, we recommended the following measures:
a. Ensure that all SEF expenditures are aligned with the approved
budget. If certain eligible expenditures are necessary but not included
in the approved budget, the LSB should prepare and secure approval
for a supplemental/realignment budget before fund utilization.
b. Require the Treasurer to consider reimbursing the SEF for the
ineligible expenditures from appropriate funding source.

1.10. We recommended and the Management agreed that:
1.10.1. The General Service Officer (GSO) fully implement the guidelines
set in COA Circular No. 2022-004 and religiously prepare the
required forms, registries, and reports to properly monitor and
safeguard Semi-Expendable Property of the Municipality;
1.10.2. The GSO submit the Report on Semi-Expendable Property Issued
(RSPI) attached with the copies of the Inventory Custodian Slips
(ICSs) and Property Acknowledgment Receipts (PARs) for
previously classified as PPE to the Municipal Accounting Office for
the recognition of expenses; and
1.10.3. The Municipal Accountant prepare the Journal Entry Voucher to
recognize the issued Semi-Expendable Property in accordance
with the submitted RSPIs and ICSs/PARs,’ as follows:
