Semi-Expendable Books 108,956.06

2.6. We recommended and the Management agreed that:
2.6.1. The Municipal General Services Office submit the fully
accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable
Property (IIRUP) to the Disposal Committee;
2.6.2. The Disposal Committee issue a certification, together with its
pertinent documents, addressed to the Municipal Accountant on
the demolition of the old public market; and
2.6.3. The Municipal Accountant prepare the following adjusting entry to
derecognize the asset from the books upon the receipt of the
certification and its supporting documents from the Disposal
Committee.
Account Debit Credit
Accumulated Depreciation – Other Structure 1,018,967.92
Prior Period Adjustments 815,092.85
Other Structure 1,834,060.77

We recommended that the LCE direct the MA to make the necessary narrative disclosures as to the amount and nature of the restricted cash balances under Note 4 – Cash and Cash Equivalents in the financial statements to ensure compliance to regulatory and legal requirements, and proper and efficient fund allocation.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2023, pp. 58-60 | The MA included said disclosure note in the financial statements.
observations.reason: Review of the CY 2024 Notes to the Financial Statements showed that restricted cash and cash equivalents amounting to ₱245,082,445.04 were properly disclosed.

observations.recommendation:
We therefore recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following: 

the MHO to furnish a copy of claims submitted to PhilHealth to the MA regularly; 



the MHO and MA to closely and regularly coordinate with PhilHealth for the reconciliation of benefit claims; and


the MA to record the corresponding receivables and income in their books.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2023
pp. 67-69 | The MHO regularly furnishes the Accounting Office with copies of PhilHealth claims submitted for proper recording.


The MHO and MA regularly coordinate with PhilHealth for reconciliation of claims and issuance of official receipts and corresponding JEVs.

Municipal Accountant took up the necessary adjusting entry.


The MA recorded the necessary adjusting entry in the books to recognize PhilHealth receivables and income.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MBO to:

Revert the savings from completed projects to the unappropriated surplus and re-appropriate the same to new PPAs allowed under the said funds; and



Seek the assistance of implementing offices like the Municipal Engineering Office and Office of the Municipal Agriculturist to come up with a menu of new PPAs that will advance the self-reliance of tobacco farmers, thereby promoting the development of the tobacco industry and to finance other LGU’s priority development projects.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2023, pp. 69-70 | Savings from prior appropriations were re-allocated through supplemental budget.




The RA 7171 and RA 8240 shares received by the LGU in the latter part of 2024 were appropriated through Appropriation Ordinance No. 2-2025 for implementation of agricultural and infrastructure projects by the Agriculture and Engineering Offices.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Development and Planning Officer to: 

Prioritize PPAs which will benefit registered tobacco farmers in line with the road map of the tobacco industry;



Conduct public consultations or assemblies with tobacco farmers duly identified by National Trade Authority to determine their current needs; and


Maximize the 25% allocation of the total share for cooperative programs, livelihood projects and financial support for registered tobacco farmers.
observations.managementAction:
Management committed to remind all members of the Municipal Development Council about the guidelines and provisions of RA 7171 and RA 8240, particularly on the proper utilization of funds.

Management gathers general input on farmers’ needs.



The Municipal Agriculture Office implemented projects for the procurement of farm supplies, equipment, and fertilizer that may help farmers in the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor request the Sangguniang Bayan to enact a Resolution authorizing the transfer of the Multi-Purpose Halls to different recipient barangays, and thereafter, prepare Deeds of Donation for the actual transfer and acceptance by the latter to substantiate the transfer of ownership of the said properties.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2022, pp. 62-64 | The Municipal Mayor requested a Resolution from the Sangguniang Bayan authorizing the transfers of these properties.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MA to further look into previous years’ files in order to account for the said receivables.  If after all the possible means were exerted and the claims could not be accounted, Management could take necessary actions for the proper write-off/derecognition of dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 in order to fairly present the Receivable accounts.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2021, pp. 68-70 | The MA has started reviewing prior years’ records to identify and validate the existence of long-outstanding receivables.


The MA requested the endorsement of a resolution for the write-off of dormant receivables, and a copy of the approved resolution was furnished to the AT.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Point System to strengthen the data gathering and collating mechanisms of the Municipality’s CBMS and GAD databases through preparation of master plan indicating the timelines, process, budget and other necessary actions.



Thereafter, start the implementation in CY 2021 in order to ensure updated, operational and reliable information provided by said databases which will serve in attaining an evidenced-based planning, policy-making, and identification of beneficiaries for relief distribution and financial assistance in the future.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020, pp. 53-56 | Management acknowledged the importance of strengthening the Municipality’s CBMS and GAD databases and has initiated discussions on how to improve the current data systems.



The Municipality has already commenced the implementation of the CBMS.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the    MSWDO    to coordinate with the MDRRM Office to revisit and revise the existing plan for the distribution of relief goods to better uphold the necessary promptness and uniformity by convening as often as possible to update the plans according to   current events.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2020, pp. 56-59 | The MSWDO updated its 2024 Manual of Operations to reflect various operational improvements, including the consolidation of local development indicators to guide relief goods distribution.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MA to record completed and on-going projects for the year it occurred regardless if paid or not.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2020, pp. 64-69 | Management acknowledged the observation and is in the process of coordinating with concerned offices to gather the necessary documents for recording the completed and ongoing NG-funded projects.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Assessor to verify whether real properties within the Municipality are properly listed and/or updated in the assessment rolls specifically those newly constructed or renovated.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2020, pp. 86-90 | The Municipal Assessor undertook verification activities and submitted updated assessment rolls for reference.

observations.recommendation: We have, therefore, recommended to the Management to strictly comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting and liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2019, pp. 89-93 | Management continues to monitor the granting and liquidation of cash advances and reminds concerned officers to comply with applicable guidelines.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan of the Municipality exercise its authority to update the existing Market and Slaughterhouse Code in conformity with the provisions of the Local Government Code of 1991, otherwise known as RA No. 7160 in order to generate additional income to finance programs and projects beneficial to the constituents.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2019, pp. 120-121 | The Sangguniang Bayan initiated the necessary legislative actions to update the Market and Slaughterhouse Code in accordance with existing laws and guidelines.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Inventory Committee to immediately convene to plan or strategize the implementation of the one-time cleansing of all PPEs owned by the Municipality in order to resolve the recurring issues on the discrepancy between the property records and accounting records and to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher to record the unrecognized depreciation on depreciable PPEs totaling P1,098,884,189.56 by debiting the Government Equity account and crediting the corresponding Accumulated Depreciation accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee has planned and strategized the implementation of the one-time cleansing of PPEs owned by the Municipality. The Municipal Accountant has also been instructed to generate and provide the supporting documents needed, if applicable, to substantiate the supposed unreconciled beginning balances of the PPE. The Municipal Engineer has been instructed to extend technical assistance to the GSO and Municipal Accountant for the validation and reconciliation of Land Improvements, Infrastructure Assets, Buildings, and Other Structures. The Inventory Committee have been directed to ensure that the RPCPPE includes all the necessary information needed and that it shall only include PPE which are physically counted, and if warranted, conduct other procedures necessary to complete the one-time cleansing of PPE.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Recognize in the General Fund books out of the Trust Fund the PPEs totaling P5,681,540.00 and thereafter record the corresponding depreciation expenses to fairly present the PPE accounts; and b. Review the Trust Fund books in the previous years to check if there have been similar cases of unrecognized PPEs and recognize them also in the General Fund books, as well as the corresponding depreciation expenses.
observations.managementAction: PPE purchased out of the Trust Fund in CY 2023 has been recognized in the General Fund books. Moreover, the corresponding depreciation expenses have been recorded. The Municipal Accountant has reviewed the Trust Fund books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Ensure that all cash advances, except for petty cash fund, are fully liquidated at year-end and any unexpended balance shall be refunded by the accountable officer; and b. Require every official who will receive cash advance to strictly observe the prescribed period of liquidation of his/her cash advance to safeguard the cash from the risk of misuse or loss.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has been directed to exert diligent efforts to recover the outstanding accountabilities from the separated employees, and to demand the settlement of all other unliquidated cash advances, and strictly enforce the timely liquidation of all cash advances by ensuring that all cash advances are fully liquidated at year-end. The Municipal Accountant has been reminded all officials and employees through an internal memorandum to observe the prescribed period of liquidation. The Municipal Accountant is preparing Tracking Sheet (RTS) for recommendations considered closed for having been reiterated with modifications.
observations.reason: Reiterated with modification in Audit Observation No. 3, Page Nos. 39 to 40, Part II of this Report. For preparation of RTS for recommendations considered closed for having been reiterated with modifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned municipal officials exercise diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of government funds by limiting the disbursements within the total income realized during the year; and that they take concrete and effective measures to cover the cash overdraft with the collections from the ensuing year.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor and other concerned municipal officials are continuously exerting efforts to properly control the disposition of funds to prevent the recurrence of cash overdraft. The Municipal Treasurer has been instructed to look into the causes of the decrease in the actual collections from Share from Economic Zones and propose specific measures to improve the financial performance of the Municipality. Municipality is continuously exerting efforts to properly control the disposition of funds to prevent the recurrence of cash overdraft.
observations.reason: Reiterated with modification in Audit Observation No. 9, Page Nos. 49 to 53, Part II of this Report. For preparation of RTS for recommendations considered closed for having been reiterated with modifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Implement the procedures for the proper disposition of the dormant accounts and thereafter facilitate the filing of the request to write off/derecognize from the books the dormant accounts pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2023-008; and b. Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of all dormant accounts in the books of the Municipality to ensure the fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has started reviewing and planning to facilitate the filing of the request for the write-off. The Municipal Accountant has conducted regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the existence of the dormant accounts in the books.
observations.reason: Not yet subjected to proper disposition. No request for write-off has been received as of December 31, 2024. For preparation of RTS for recommendations considered closed for having been reiterated with modifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and Municipal Accountant to meticulously verify and ensure the Performance Security and Warranty Security submitted by the winning bidders were dated and valid within the prescribed periods in order to fully protect the interest of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The BAC and the Municipal Accountant has been ensuring that the securities were dated and valid within the prescribed periods.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Services Officer to: a. Submit the duly accomplished PIF to the Office of the Supervising Auditor or the Audit Team Leader and the GIF of GSIS on or before April 30 of each year to be used as basis for the assessment of the general insurance coverage of all the Municipality’s insurable properties; and b. Insure with the GIF of GSIS all the Municipality’s insurable properties with the appropriate insurance coverage to protect the Municipality thru indemnification in cases of damage or loss due to fortuitous events. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to include in the annual budget the allocation of sufficient appropriation for the payment of insurance premiums.
observations.managementAction: The GSO has submitted the PIF to the Office of the Supervising Auditor and GIF of GSIS. The GSO has insured with the GIF of GSIS all the Municipality’s insurable vehicles. The Municipal Budget Officer has included in the annual budget a sufficient appropriation for the payment of insurance premiums.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the Local Finance Committee to analyze trends in tax revenue collections from prior years, determine the amount of income with reasonable assurance of collectability, and use these data as basis or benchmark in preparing the Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year; b. Require Municipal Treasurer to intensify the conduct tax campaign initiatives to boost the collection and promote prompt payment of taxes due to the Municipality; c. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare the Daily Cash Position Report and monitor the availability of cash to prioritize expenditures to avoid the incurrence of overdraft; and d. Direct the Local Finance Committee to adopt measures to eliminate the cash overdraft by exercising diligence, prudence, and proper control in the Annual Budget and disposition of the Municipality’s funds, limit expenditures within the total income collected and realized during the year, and reduce unnecessary operating expenditures until the cash overdraft is eliminated.
observations.managementAction: The Local Finance Committee has been directed to study and analyze trends in tax revenue collections from prior years, determine the amount of income with reasonable assurance of collectability, and use these data as basis in the preparation of the Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year. The Municipal Treasurer has been directed to intensify the conduct tax campaign initiatives to boost collection and promote prompt payment of taxes due to the Municipality. The Municipal Treasurer has also been instructed to prepare Daily Cash Position Report and monitor the availability of cash to prioritize expenditures to avoid incurrence of overdraft. The Local Finance Committee has been directed to adopt measures to eliminate the cash overdraft through diligence, prudence, and proper control in the Annual Budget and disposition of the Municipality’s funds, to limit expenditures within the total income collected and realized during the year, and reduce unnecessary operating expenditures until the cash overdraft is eliminated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from processing and granting additional cash advance/s to AOs unless their previous cash advances have been fully accounted for or liquidated as provided in Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and Item No. 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002, and issue the required certification before granting any cash advance in compliance with Item no. 1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and b. Require every AO who will receive any cash advance to strictly observe the prescribed liquidation period of his/her cash advance consistent with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002, and Section 109(A)(1) of LTOM 2nd Edition, 2019, Book III.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has been directed to refrain from processing and granting additional cash advance/s to AOs unless their previous cash advances have been fully liquidated, and to require all AOs who will receive any cash advance to strictly observe the prescribed liquidation period as provided under COA Circular No. 97-002.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant, Human Resource Management Officer and other concerned officials to ensure that: a. The ToR of individual consultants whose works are either highly technical or proprietary or primarily confidential or policy determining, where trust and confidence are the primary consideration, and the justification for their hiring are prepared and submitted by the end-user units before procurement or negotiation; b. Validation of consultants legal, technical and financial capability and pertinent recommendation of award have been made by the BAC, pursuant to the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and c. Submit justification on the necessity of hiring consultants and lacking documents, and ensure that the Accomplishment Reports submitted are supported by detailed information and tangible outputs/reports.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and Human Resource Officer have been directed to ensure that: a. The ToR of individual consultants and justifications for their hiring are prepared and submitted before procurement or negotiation; b. That consultants have been validated by the BAC and has pertinent recommendation of award; and c. The hiring of consultants is justified and that submitted Accomplishment Reports are supported with detailed information and tangible outputs/reports.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all DVs and their supporting documents are duly stamped “PAID” after payment is made to prevent the possible re-use of the same on similar future transactions in line with COA Circular No. 92-389 dated November 3, 1992.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer has been directed to ensure that all DVs and their corresponding supporting documents are duly stamped “PAID” after payment is made in line with COA Circular No. 92-389.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Market Supervisor, Fish Port Supervisor, and Municipal Legal Officer to facilitate the execution of contracts between the Municipality and existing Rosario Public Market and Fish Port stallholders, as implied by the pertinent provisions of the Tax Code of the Municipality of Rosario.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Market Supervisor, Fish Port Supervisor, and Municipal Legal officer have been directed to facilitate the renewal of contracts between the Municipality and existing Rosario Public Market and Fish Port stallholders.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Local Finance Committee to propose fiscal policies and measures aimed at improving the financial performance of the Municipality; and b. Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the expenditures, ensuring they do not exceed the total income realized during the year to avoid incurrence of net loss.
observations.managementAction: The Local Finance Committee has been directed to propose fiscal policies and measures aimed at improving the financial performance of the Municipality. The Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant have also been directed to closely monitor the expenditure so as not to exceed the total income realized during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor : a. Direct all concern Municipality Officials and Employees to stop the procurement and/or payments for goods and services through reimbursements, except those allowable or emergency needs, and adhere strictly to Section 93 of PD No. 1445 which requires government agencies to make direct payments to suppliers, contractors and other payees are made in checks to ensure transparency and accountability of government transactions; and b. Ensure that taxes on government money payments are properly withheld and remitted to the BIR and charge to the personal account of the employee responsible for the withholding and remittances of the tax the interest, penalties and surcharges that may be incurred for non-compliance with the above-mentioned BIR regulations.
observations.managementAction: We will review our existing policies and processes regarding the procurement and/or payments for goods and services through reimbursements. Please note however, that due to some exigent circumstances for example for gasoline expense, in cases of late-night travel or out of town activities (meeting/trainings/seminars/rescue operation, etc.), the procurement of goods through reimbursements cannot be avoided. The Municipal Accountant has also been directed to ensure the proper withholding and remittance of taxes to the BIR and to charge to the personal account of the employee responsible the interest, penalties and surcharges that may be incurred in case of non-compliance to the corresponding BIR regulations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the implementation of these courses of actions:
a. The Municipal Treasurer’s Office faithfully and diligently update the register and include only those released checks in the Monthly Report of Check issued for the submission to the Accounting Office; and
b. The Municipal Accountant record the transaction only upon the receipt of the RCI from the Treasury Office and effect the same in monthly bank reconciliation as adjustments.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and personnel in-charge of the maintenance of the check register will conduct meeting with personnel in charge of the maintenance of the check register. The Municipal Accountant to record the transaction only upon the receipt of the RCI from the Treasury Office. The Municipal Accountant will conduct meeting with the accounting bookkeepers for recording of transaction upon the receipt of RCI and effect the same in monthly bank reconciliation as adjustments.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of actions:
a. The concerned offices receiving equipment from other agencies inform the heads of offices on the Property Transfer Report and/or secure copies of Deeds of Donations and/or Deed of donations and coordinate the same with the Municipal General Services Office and Municipal Accounting Office for proper recording and property tagging; and particularly to MAO, MHO and MENRO.
b. The Inventory Committee to be diligent in the conduct of physical count of all properties of the LGU and subsequently prepare and submit the RCPPE; and
c. The Municipal Engineering Office to prepare Certificate of Completion, Final Inspection Report and other related documents for supporting the completed projects and forward these documents to the Accounting Office for them to properly reclassify the total costs of all completed projects from CIP accounts to the appropriate PPE accounts; and the Municipal Accountant to compute accurate depreciation expenses for all recorded properties.
observations.managementAction: During meeting of Office heads, the concerned offices shall inform the head of offices on the Property Transfer Report and secure copies of Deeds of Donations of Property, and coordinate the same with the Municipal General Services Office and Municipal Accountant for recording of assets particularly to MAO, MHO and MENRO. The Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all properties of the LGU and prepare and submit the RCPPE. The Municipal Engineer to prepare the required contract documents for the completed projects and forward these documents to the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of action:
a. The Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Treasurer and compute the appropriate liquidated damages for the delayed completion of the project – improvement of drainage canal and final billing.
b. The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant cause the immediate return of the unexpended balances to the Bureau of Treasury in compliance with the budget circulars governing each flagship program.
observations.managementAction: Instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Treasurer to compute the liquidated damages for the delayed completion of the project – improvement of drainage canal and final billing. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate return of unexpended balances to the Bureau of Treasury in compliance with budget circulars governing each flagship program.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to include in the LDRRMF Investment Plan during budgeting process, a separate caption for the list of programs, projects and activities to be charged under the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in accordance with Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
observations.managementAction: To include in the LDRRMF Investment Plan during budgeting process, a separate caption for the list of programs, projects and activities identified in the LDRRMF Investment Plan.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of actions:
a. Install controls and strategies so that the prepared checks will be issued promptly to its respective payees to avoid staling; charge on the released of financial documents from MTO to MAO to avoid loss and misplaced documents; and
b. Strictly follow the proper submission of paid vouchers to Accounting Office. Reiterated in this report.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to conduct meeting with their personnel in-charge of the maintenance of the check register with submission to Accounting Office; The Municipal Accountant to be consistent in submitting complete DVs with its supporting documents on time for the conduct of judicious audit and verification thereof.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of actions:
a. Require the contractor to rectify the noted deficiency otherwise, the same amount be disallowed in audit; and
b. Diligently conduct final inspection of all completed projects before accepting to avoid payments resulting loss to the government.
observations.managementAction: Require the contractor to rectify the noted deficiency otherwise, the same amount be disallowed in audit; Conduct final inspection of completed projects before acceptance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of actions:
a. The Municipal Engineering Office to conduct thorough assessment of the condition of the building to determine the extent of damage and require the contractor to do additional works for areas that need remedial actions; and
b. Prioritize allocation of funds for the establishment of access roads that will lead to the immediate utilization and operation of these two infrastructure projects.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineering Office to conduct thorough assessment of the condition of the building (Slaughterhouse and Public Terminal) and determine the extent of damage. Allocate funds in 2025 for the establishment of access roads.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of actions:
a. Prioritize the allocation of funds for the installation of power supply at the Post-Harvest Residual Facility in Mahayahay, Sta. Cruz in order to utilize the waste shredder and composter; and
b. Send letter to the source agency for the operating manual of the infant radiant warmer for it to be functional.
observations.managementAction: To transfer the waste shredder and composter to the Post-Harvest Facility in Mahayahay, Sta. Cruz in order to utilize however remain unutilized due to no allocation for electricity this year. Send letter to World Health Organization (WHO), the source agency for the operating manual of transferred infant radiant warmer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management implement the following course of actions:
To the Municipal Treasurer:
a. Maintain a Real Property Tax Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card and update the same yearly; b. Compute the RPT and SET Receivable based on the RPT Register/Taxpayer’s Index; c. Ensure that the amount of the delinquent taxes to be incorporated in the records and reconcile the delinquent list versus per Barangay and per individual; d. Trace and reconcile the difference of ₱68,073,258.88 between the lists of real property tax delinquencies summarized per barangay and versus per individual taxpayers; e. Submit the certified list of delinquent taxpayers to the Accounting Office every beginning of the year.
To the Municipal Accountant:
f. Ensure that the delinquent taxes and the report submitted by the MTO are complete, sufficient and reconciled in order to achieve the accurate figure to recognize.
observations.managementAction: Assign focal person to handle and maintain Real Property Register. Compute the amount of the delinquent taxes to be submitted to the Municipal Accountant; Ensure the delinquent taxes submitted by the MTO are complete, sufficient and reconciled.
observations.reason: Only one programmer accommodating the system used by all the whole Province. The submitted list was not per barangay. The MTO failed to submit accurate and conclusive list because source data, the system generated list had not been cleaned by the programmer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
a. Direct those concerned officials to meticulously prepare and review the Approved Budget for the Contracts (ABC), Detailed Unit Price Analysis (DUPA), and other related documents to ensure that the quantity meets the requirements of the plan and the costs are derived accurately to attain a reasonable estimate; b. Require the persons liable to refund the deficiencies noted; and c. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to monitor the refund by persons liable to cater the deficiencies.
observations.managementAction: Direct concerned officials to prepare and review the ABC, DUPA and other related documents; Require persons liable to refund the deficiencies noted; Instruct the Municipal Accountant to monitor the refund by persons liable.
observations.reason: No technical inspection could be thoroughly conducted due to non-submission of documents as follows: As-built plan of the Portland Cement Concrete Pavement showing the phases of the project that were implemented, Tabulated total area of the PCCP considered irregular in shape.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following:
 
Municipal Accountant to ensure that all total costs incurred for all projects, programs and activities are accrued accordingly and that obligations are completely and adequately recognized in the SCBAA; 
 
Municipal Budget Officer to update the Statement of Appropriations and Obligations and ensure that all obligations for the year are reflected therein; and

OIC-Municipal Engineer to conduct a mandatory inspection at year-end with duly certified inspection reports to support the percentage of completion reported in the Status Report on Quarterly PPAs for all ongoing projects for the Municipal Accountant to accrue the same in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to record the infrastructure in their books first to reflect in the financial records the transactions made and properly reflect them in the financial statements. 
 
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor request the Sangguniang Bayan to enact a Resolution authorizing the transfer of the Multi-Purpose Halls to different recipient barangays, and thereafter, prepare Deeds of Donation for the actual transfer and acceptance by the latter to substantiate the transfer of ownership of the said properties.

The Municipal Mayor requested a Resolution from Sangguniang Bayan authorizing the transfers of these properties.

observations.recommendation:

Bids and Awards Committee to remind prospective bidders during the pre-bid conference of the completeness of information in the required Class "A" documents, particularly the completeness of declared ongoing and awarded but not yet started government and private contracts and the accuracy of declared NFCC. TWG to conduct bid evaluation and post- qualification diligently in accordance with pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184 and its IRR, taking into consideration the key personnel assigned to the projects.

TWG assured the Audit Team to conduct bid evaluation and post- qualification diligently in accordance with pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184 and its IRR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to stop their practice of deducting certain amount on the suppliers’ claim as “contractor’s tax” and ensure that deductions are in accordance with valid and existing laws or ordinances.

the GSO and Municipal Accountant make collaborative procedures in order to sort any record discrepancy/ies, and to update their respective records as well; and


the Disposal Committee to expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties to free up storage spaces, prevent further deterioration of PPE and generate additional income from sale; and

Enforcement of the collection on the underdeclared sales with corresponding interest, surcharges and penalties.

Conducted regular inspection of financial records of business establishments.


Maintained periodic reviews to ascertain whether collection targets are attained.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Point System to strengthen the data gathering and collating mechanisms of the Municipality’s CBMS and GAD databases through preparation of master plan indicating the timelines, process, budget and other necessary actions.

Thereafter, start the implementation in CY 2021 in order to ensure updated, operational and reliable information provided by said databases which will serve in attaining an evidenced-based planning, policy-making, and identification of beneficiaries for relief distribution and financial assistance in the future.
observations.managementAction:
Coordinated with the PSA to fast-track the remaining activities and training of the CBMS.



Implementation of CBMS for the ensuing years.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the    MSWDO    to coordinate with the MDRRM Office to revisit and revise the existing plan for the distribution of relief goods to better uphold the necessary promptness and uniformity by convening as often as possible to update the plans according to   current events.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor directed the    MSWDO    to coordinate with the MDRRM Office to revisit and revise the existing plans for the distribution of relief goods.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to improve internal control process of settlement of AP and to furnish report as a result hereto which may include provision of additional manpower, among others.

the Municipal Accountant to:

record completed and on-going projects to the year it occurred regardless if paid or not;

Maintained records of PPEs

Maintained copies of MOA.

the Municipal Accountant to:

complete the data requirements in the Journals and Journal Entry Vouchers and to certify correct them; and

use the General Journal and the Cash Receipts Journal for the purpose these were created.

The cash advances from prior years cannot be settled due to the death of the responsible person.

Management requested for an SB Resolution requesting COA to write-off/ derecognize the dormant Cash Advance accounts.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan of the municipality exercise its authority to update the existing Market and Slaughterhouse Code in conformity with the provisions of the Local Government Code of 1991, otherwise known as RA No. 7160 in order to generate additional income to finance programs and projects beneficial to the constituents.
observations.managementAction: The Sangguniang Bayan of the municipality exercised its authority to update the existing Market and Slaughterhouse Code in conformity with the provisions of the Local Government Code of 1991

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Inventory Committee to conduct the one-time cleansing of all the PPEs owned by the Municipality in accordance with the guidelines and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 in order to resolve the recurring issues on the discrepancy between the property records and accounting records and to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Review its PPE records and ascertain the source of discrepancy between the general ledger balances of PPE accounts and the corresponding schedule of PPEs; and b. Draw Journal Entry Voucher to record the unrecognized depreciation on depreciable PPEs totaling P952,869,527.11 by debiting the Government Equity account and crediting the corresponding Accumulated Depreciation accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee is on its final stage of the physical inventory of all PPEs owned by the Municipality to reconcile with the accounting records for the implementation of the one-time cleansing of PPE. Further, the Municipal Accountant has been directed to record the unrecognized depreciation on depreciable PPEs of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: Finalize Inventory Committee to conduct the one-time cleansing of all PPEs owned by the Municipality in order to resolve the recurring issues on the discrepancy between the property records and accounting records and to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Review its PPE records and ascertain the source of discrepancy between the general ledger balances of PPE accounts and the corresponding Schedule of PPEs; and b. Draw Journal Entry Voucher to record the unrecognized depreciation on depreciable PPEs totaling P952,869,527.11 by debiting the Government Equity account and crediting the corresponding Accumulated Depreciation accounts.
observations.managementAction: The GSO with the Engineering Department - One-time cleansing is not yet implemented. The Accounting Office headed by the Municipal Accountant has reviewed PPE records to ascertain the source of discrepancies between the PPEs; and the Municipal Accountant has been directed to draw Journal Entry Voucher to record the unrecognized depreciation on depreciable PPEs of the Municipality.
observations.reason: One-time cleansing is not yet implemented.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Mayor to direct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all properties owned by the municipality and prepare and submit the RPCPPE in prescribed format not later than January 31 of each year, and
observations.managementAction: Follow audit recommendations

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management prepare the Capacity Development Agenda and the Local Chief Executive should ensure that seminars and conventions attended is responsive to the organizational result and development outcome, keeping in mind the prudent use of the scarce financial resources of the LGU. Also, before approving the authority to attend any seminar-workshop the authority and competencies of the sponsoring organization/agency and its RPs should be accordingly verified.
observations.managementAction: Prepare the Capacity Development Agenda
observations.reason: Delayed preparation of the CapDev. Lack of personnel in the HRMO to prepare the database of the training needs and attended by all the LGU Officials/ employees. Misconception of the Official/ employees that this training is mandatory.

observations.recommendation: The GFPS should strictly observe the timelines in the preparation and submission of the GPB and Accomplishment Reports to DILG and other oversight agencies, and the endorsed copies thereof be furnished to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: Formulate a GAD code, prepare a GAD accomplishment report
observations.reason: Did not create a GAD code due to lack of manpower from MSWDO

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Compute and consistently provide the correct depreciation for the road network system of which no residual value shall be recognized;
Prepare the necessary adjusting entry as presented below:
Depreciation Expense        			 ₱798,423.80
Prior Period Adjustment					 314,127.67
Accumulated Depreciation Expense      		₱1,112,551.48
Restate the CY 2023 consolidated financial statements by reflecting the correct depreciation expenses for CYs 2017 to 2023 in all the affected components of the financial statements.
Municipal Accountant will make the necessary adjusting entry to reflect the correct depreciation expenses for Local Road Networks.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to:
GSO-Designate to prepare and maintain a property cards for each of the PPE accounts on the basis of the AIR and PAR;
Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain property ledger cards;
GSO-Designate and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their own property records; and
Inventory Committee to conduct annual inventory of PPE.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive to:
Create an Inventory Committee, who are temporarily relieve of all their regular duties, to complete the physical inventory of PPEs in three months or less;
Direct the Inventory Committee to prepare PIP, containing at least the specific assignments/duties of the committee members, the cut-off date and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from the start-up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory;
Submit the approved PIP to the COA Audit Team at least ten calendar days before the start of the scheduled inventory-taking activities; and
Ensure full implementation of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts to verify the existence, condition and accountability of the PPE.

We therefore recommended that the LCE direct the GSO and Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain property records for each supply or commodity to facilitate proper inventory management system.

We also recommended that the LCE direct the following:
The GSO to:
Spearhead the conduct of physical count of inventories on all supplies in the stockrooms every June 30 and December to verify the existence of recorded balances;
Furnish the Municipal Accountant and Department Office/Heads copies of RPCI to facilitate reconciliation of records; and
Reconcile records with the subsidiary ledgers of the Municipal Accountant to ensure the accuracy and completeness of records maintained;
Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare the correcting entry to adjust the ending inventory accounts as follows:
General Fund books
Debit: Drugs and Medicines for Distribution		 ₱ 157,695.22
Debit: Agricultural and Marine Supplies Expense           2,995,635.00
Debit: Welfare Goods Inventory		     109,097.00
Credit: Drugs and Medicines Expenses				 ₱ 157,695.22
Agricultural and Marine Supplies for Distribution	 2,995,635.00
Welfare Goods Expense				 109,097.00
Special Fund Books:
Debit: Office Supplies Expense			       ₱ 327,690.00
Credit: Office Supplies Inventory			₱ 327,690.00
Reconcile and update subsidiary ledgers with the stock cards and RPCI as of June 30 and December 31 to accurately present supplies in stock; and
Maintain the perpetual inventory records comprising of Supplies Ledger Cards for each commodity/stock.
Municipal Health Officer and Municipal Agricultural Office to regularly furnish the GSO with inventory reports, and coordinate with the GSO and Municipal Accountant for the timely reconciliation of inventory records.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary narrative disclosures as to the amount and nature of the restricted cash balances under Note 4 – Cash and Cash Equivalents in the financial statements to ensure compliance to regulatory and legal requirements, and proper and efficient fund allocation.

The Municipal Accountant commented that she will make the necessary narrative disclosures.

We recommended that the LCE direct the following:
Department heads concerned to make representations from the source agencies to seek assistance in the liquidation of the said funds; and
Municipal Accountant to adjust the amount of ₱28,875.00 pertaining to the difference between the recorded balance of the ICFP – LGSEF in the financial statements and the report of liquidation and disbursements.

Additionally, we recommended that the LCE direct:

a. the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the Cash-in-Bank – Fire Code Fees and Due to NGAs – Fire Code Fees ledger balances and afterwards, take up the following entries to properly recognize the receipt of Share from Fire Code fees in CY 2023:
Trust Fund books:
DR: Due to NGAs				    ₱753,584.36
CR: Cash in Bank – Fire Code Fees		₱753,584.36
General Fund books:
DR: Cash in Bank – Fire Code Fees		₱753,584.36
CR: Share from National Wealth		₱753,584.36

the Municipal Budget Officer and Bureau of Fire Protection Officer to facilitate the appropriation and utilization, respectively, of the 20 percent share on fire code fees for the improvement and maintenance of fire stations.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Stop the practice of withholding business taxes from payments to contractors/suppliers since these two are different and distinct from one another, instead collect business taxes on or before January 20th of the subsequent year; and
Send demand letters to the business address of the contractors/suppliers prior to the deadline of payment to give them time to comply.
Subsequently, we recommended that the LCE initiate the revision of their tax ordinance, through the Sanggunian, to include provisions for the advance collection of business taxes.
Lastly, we recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to adjust the affected accounts in the financial statements with the following correcting journal entry:
Business Tax			₱885,585.56
Other Deferred Credits		₱885,585.56

Management will stop the practice of deducting business taxes from the payment of projects. Further, they will revisit their revenue code and include the provision for the advance collection of business taxes. While the Municipal Accountant took note of the recommendation and will comply.

We therefore recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following:
the Municipal Health Officer to furnish a copy of claims submitted to PhilHealth to the Municipal Accountant regularly;
the Municipal Health Officer and Municipal Accountant to closely and regularly coordinate with PhilHealth for the reconciliation of benefit claims; and
the Municipal Accountant to record the corresponding receivables and income in their books.

The Municipal Health Officer assured the Audit Team that copy of the claims will be forwarded to the Municipal Accountant and coordination with the PhilHealth will be implemented. The Municipal Accountant ensured that the receivable and income will be recorded in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to:
revert the savings from completed projects to the unappropriated surplus and re-appropriate the same to new PPAs allowed under the said funds; and
seek the assistance of implementing offices like the Municipal Engineering Office and Office of the Municipal Agriculturist to come up with a menu of new PPAs that will advance the self-reliance of tobacco farmers, thereby promoting the development of the tobacco industry and to finance other LGU’s priority development projects.

Management commented that they will identify development projects/programs that will be incorporated in their Comprehensive Development Plan which will be presented to the Municipal Development Council meeting for its approval and adoption.

With the foregoing, we therefore recommended that the LCE direct the GSO Designate to:
Conduct inventory of all insurable properties together with the member of the Inventory Committee; and
Prepare complete lists of insurable properties using PIF to facilitate the processing of insurance with the GSIS.
We also recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Budget Officer to include in the ensuing years' budget preparation the appropriation of sufficient amount for the insurance covering all insurable properties of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Development and Planning Officer to:
Prioritize PPAs which will benefit registered tobacco farmers in line with the road map of the tobacco industry;
Conduct public consultations or assemblies with tobacco farmers duly identified by National Trade Authority to determine their current needs; and
Maximize the 25 percent allocation of the total share for cooperative programs, livelihood projects and financial support for registered tobacco farmers.

We therefore recommended that LCE direct the GFPS to:
Reinforce the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget with a more prudent gender analysis through establishing the cause/s of identified gender issues and thereafter formulate the PPAs that directly address the same; and
For instances when only a portion of a PPA is aligned with GAD, consider attributing the eligible portion through administering the Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines while the non-eligible portion should be charged under an appropriate fund.

Management, through the GFPS, ensured that they will be more prudent in the preparation of GAD Plan and Budget so as to optimize the GAD fund and achieve the objective of gender advocacy and women empowerment.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
a. prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as soon as possible
by creating an Inventory Committee composed of adequate number of members to
be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less;
b. cause the revision of the submitted PIP to include the specific name of committee
members, the cut-off date and the schedule specifying the dates and locations of
the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the
physical inventory which shall be within three months or less and duly approved
by the Municipal Mayor to be submitted to the Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar
days before the scheduled inventory taking activities;
c. instruct the newly created Inventory Committee, GSO and the Accounting Unit to
perform the preliminary activities by preparation of appropriate reports such as
latest RPCPPE, Inventory Count Form and PPELCs and reconcile their records
before the actual physical inventory;
d. command the Municipal Accountant to verify the validity of the items listed on her
lapsing schedule versus the NTFS for Road Networks, Water Supply Systems and
Other Structures and accordingly make the necessary adjusting entry thereof; and
e. provide transportation equipment and other necessary tools for the implementation
of the one-time cleansing.

We strongly reiterated that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to conduct the immediate
reconciliation of the individual balances of the Cash in Bank-Local Currency,
Current Account balances with the respective cashbook/s and make the necessary
adjustment/s thereon;
b. Municipal Treasurer to verify the status of the stale checks as of December 31, 2023
with its payee to determine their actual existence for replacement, if applicable, and
comply with the procedures in the handling of stale/spoiled checks as stated in
Section 59 of the Manual on NGAS; and
c. Municipal Treasurer to ensure that checks issued in CY 2024 will be monitored and
that payees are duly notified of their outstanding checks at least one month before
they become stale.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. to exert more effort to identify the sources of the unreconciled difference between
the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds through reconciliation of the
accounts; and
b. otherwise, resort to prescribed procedures on write-off of receivables and/or fund
transfers upon presentation of the required documentation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
prepare adjusting Journal Entry Voucher debiting the Prior Period Adjustment account
and crediting the Accumulated Depreciation – Road Networks account totalling
P2,205,205.33 and stop the practice of providing residual value for the depreciable
component of the road network systems in accordance with Item 14, Section IV of COA
Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor and Municipal
Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents required under
Section 13.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and justification on the difference on the
purpose of the purchase of lot indicated on documents to properly substantiate the above
disbursement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor immediately demand the
supplier/contractor to provide the missing components of the IT infrastructure totaling
P410,000.00 in full conformity with the specifications in the contract at no additional
cost.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor collect appropriate liquidated
damages from the defaulting supplier/contractor, otherwise the subject payment may
be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Budget Officer to include in the appropriations the fund for the
application of insurance of all the insurable properties;
b. GSO-OIC to prepare and submit the consolidated Property Inventory Form (PIF)
listing of all the insurable properties and other assets to GSIS for their evaluation
and approval, copy furnish the Office of the Auditor, in compliance to Section 5 of
RA No. 656 and COA Circular 2018-002; and
c. Municipal Accountant to verify and obtain information as regards the L300
ambulance with plate no. SJD 402 and recognize the same in the book of accounts
if applicable. Henceforth, the GSO-OIC ensure that the properties to be insured are
legally owned by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from processing disbursement vouchers
for a new cash advance of an employee until their previous cash advance has been
settled and issue a certification that the previous cash advance has been fully
liquidated and accounted for in the books, for attachment to succeeding
193
disbursement vouchers for the grant of cash advances, pursuant to Item 1.1, Annex
A of COA Circular 2023-004; and
b. designate an adequate number of disbursing officers for cash advance who will be
able to handle numerous cash advances of the Municipality one at a time, or if none
is available, request to the Sangguniang Bayan the creation of new position(s) in
the Municipal Treasurer’s Office.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. MPDC Office and Municipal Engineering Office to identify and propose priority
projects under the 20% Development Fund that are ready for procurement and
implementation; and
b. members of the Local Development Council to review if the projects under the
Continuing Funds of the 20 percent DF are still implementable, otherwise, revert the
unexpended amount of P4,242,351.07 to the unappropriated surplus of the General
Fund and reprogram to priority projects of the Municipality that are likewise
procurement-and-implementation ready.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the 100 percent
implementation of planned activities under the 20 percent Development Fund
considering that funds are available for these activities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. MDRRMC to evaluate the feasibility of planned PPAs, especially those held under
Continuing Appropriations, and identify projects which will no longer be
implemented for possible reversion and reprogramming to other disaster-related
PPAs; and
b. Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to present to the MDRRMC the
savings from completed projects in CY 2022 and previous years for appropriation to
other disaster preparedness and mitigation PPAs of the Municipality in the
succeeding year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive
Committee together with the Gender Focal Point System and the Technical Working
Group to:
a. exert active efforts in accomplishing all the targeted and planned client-focused
and organization-focused PPAs; and
b. coordinate with the Municipal Human Resources Management Officer to require all
members of the GFPS including elected local officials and department heads to
undergo capacity building programs on GAD which shall include but not be limited
to: gender sensitivity training (GST), gender-responsive planning and budgeting,
gender analysis, gender audit and GAD tools such as the Gender Mainstreaming
Evaluation Framework (GMEF), Gender Responsive LGU Ka Ba (GeRL Ka Ba) Self-
Assessment Tool, Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) and
other succeeding GAD tools.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:
a. require the GAD Focal Point System to prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with
Section 4.C.2, paragraph 1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular
(JMC) No. 2013-01 and Item 5, paragraph 6 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-
01; and
b. require the GAD Focal Point System to secure first the Certificate of Endorsement
of the Annual GAD Plan and Budget from the DILG Provincial Office before
incorporating to the Municipality’s Annual Budget the planned PPAs to properly
ascertain the relevance of the gender issues identified and gender-responsiveness of
the PPAs indicated in the GPB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Engineering Office to ensure the implementation, within the prescribed
timeline, of the succeeding projects to be funded by the LGSF-SBDP of the NTF-
ELCAC;
b. Bids and Award Committee to require future contract awardees for the submission
of performance bond with proper period of validity pursuant to Section 39.3 of the
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
c. Municipal Local Finance Committee to strictly comply with the posting and
reporting requirements in the utilization of LGSF-SBDP of the NTF-ELCAC,
pursuant to Section 5 of DBM LBC No. 151.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Administrator to
countersign all the checks drawn by the Municipal Treasurer excluding those checks
charged against the SB budget pursuant to the above cited regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to increase
the fidelity bond of the said accountable officer to cover the total daily accountabilities
in accordance with the schedule provided under Treasury Circular No. 02-2019.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the withdrawal of SDO’s
designation as collecting officer and refrain from designating functions of collecting
and disbursing to the same person to promote proper segregation of duties for a sound
internal control system on cash management.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledger for Cash-Local Treasury for
each Collecting Officer and update them daily; and
b. Municipal Treasurer to require all Collecting Officers to maintain Cashbook for each
fund to record their collections and remittances daily.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to immediately convene to plan or strategize the implementation of the one-time cleansing of all PPEs owned by the Municipality in order to resolve the recurring issues on the discrepancy between the property records and accounting records and to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher to record the unrecognized depreciation on depreciable PPEs totaling P1,098,884,189.56 by debiting the Government Equity account and crediting the corresponding Accumulated Depreciation accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Recognize in the General Fund books the PPEs totaling P5,681,540.00 purchased out of the Trust Fund in CY 2023 and thereafter record also the corresponding depreciation expenses to fairly present the affected PPE accounts; and
b. Review the Trust Fund books in the previous years to check if there have been similar cases of unrecognized PPEs and recognize them also in the General Fund books, as well as the corresponding depreciation expenses.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Ensure that all cash advances, except for petty cash fund, are fully liquidated at year-end and any unexpended balance shall be refunded by the accountable officer; and
b. Require every official who will receive cash advance to strictly observe the prescribed period of liquidation of his/her cash advance to safeguard the cash from the risk of misuse or loss.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and other concerned municipal officials to investigate the cause/s of the substantial decrease in the actual collections from Share from Economic Zones and propose specific measures to improve the financial performance of the Municipality, benchmarking from its performance in prior years, and to furnish the Audit Team with a written report thereon for validation purpose.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned municipal officials exercise diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of government funds by limiting the disbursements within the total income realized during the year to prevent the recurrence of cash overdraft. It is likewise recommended that they take concrete and effective measures to cover the cash overdraft with the collections from the ensuing year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Implement the procedures for the proper disposition of the dormant accounts totaling P5,181,600.34 and thereafter facilitate the filing of the request to write-off/derecognize from the books the dormant accounts pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2023-008; and
b. Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of all dormant accounts in the books of the Municipality to ensure the fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and Municipal Accountant to meticulously verify and ensure that the Performance Security and Warranty Security submitted by the winning bidders were dated and valid within the prescribed periods in order to fully protect the interest of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Services Officer to:
a. Submit the duly accomplished PIF to the Office of the Supervising Auditor or Audit Team Leader and the GIF of GSIS on or before April 30 of each year to be used as basis for the assessment of the general insurance coverage of all the Municipality’s insurable properties; and
b. Insure with the GIF of GSIS all the Municipality’s insurable assets and properties with the appropriate insurance coverage to protect the Municipality thru indemnification in cases of damage or loss due to fortuitous events.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to include in the annual budget the allocation of sufficient appropriation for the payment of insurance premiums in order to ensure that all the Municipality’s insurable properties will be properly insured with the GSIS.
None.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Sangguniang Bayan and
the Finance Offices:

a. Implement a monitoring system tailored to the specific financial
processes and requirements of each fund, ensuring real time tracking
of funds, which should include both the appropriation and its
obligation and actual cash inflows and outflows.

b. Establish clear procedures for monitoring financial transactions,
including regular reconciliations and reviews to identify and rectify
discrepancies promptly.

c. Review previous years’ transactions and identify the specific purpose
and/or sources of funds of the balances in the 20% DF and
LDRRMF.

d. Prepare the necessary Utilization and/or Liquidation Reports on all
funds.

We recommended the following corrective measures and/or actions to be
undertaken to ensure compliance with the legal and documentary
requirements for the three projects:

a) The Municipal Mayor to request from the Sangguniang Bayan
authority to enter into agreement with the lot owners to obtain access
to the infrastructure for the operation of the pig houses which are
constructed in the gated premises of the donor, and submit the same to
the audit team together with the authority to enter into the
Contract/Deed of Donation. The agreement with the private owner
should outline the terms and conditions of the project, including the
responsibilities, timelines and compensation arrangements, if any.

b) The Municipal Agriculturist to follow up and request from BFAR the
immediate processing and evaluation of the application for permit for
both the establishment and operation of the fishpond and fish corrals.
Henceforth, ensure that the necessary engineering surveys should be
conducted and the appropriate permits and authority be obtained before
the implementation of any project/undertaking.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the recipient
barangays to submit the status report on the implementation of the
requested project to be funded out of the 20% DF. Henceforth, draw a
MOA in every undertaking with the BLGU providing for the reporting
requirements, implementation period and return of any unused funds.

Further, require the Finance Committee to ensure that all development
projects that are funded out of the 20% DF should comply with the
requirements in DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Engineer, MPDC, and the Bids and Awards Committee to monitor the
contractors’ performance of the construction activities. Likewise, enforce
the remedies set forth under Republic Act 9184 on defaulting contractors
which includes the termination of the contract, imposition of liquidation
damages and/or recommendation to blacklist the contractor with negative
slippage.

We recommended the following measures to the Local Chief Executive:

a) Create a monitoring or inspectorate team to make preliminary
inspection and prepare and submit a report thereon before the
turnover and acceptance of any project.

b) Direct the MPDC and Municipal Engineer to intensify the monitoring
and coordination of all on-going projects to minimize defects and
ensure that the progress of construction are within the timetable
provided in the agreed construction works. Likewise, instruct the
Municipal Engineer to immediately inspect the completed and on-
going projects and prepare the computation of liquidated damages.

c) Require the Municipal Accountant to appropriately scrutinize the
completeness of the documents attached supporting each claim which
should include the verification of the validity and regularity of the
documents.

d) Enforce the sanctions and penalties to defaulting contractors, such as
imposition of administrative suspension for one to two years from
participating in all the government bidding processes, termination of the
contract, and forfeiture of the performance security and enforcement
of the liquidated damages, as provided for in Republic Act 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Engineer and MPDO to examine the reason and validity of the work
suspension and/or extent of the delay and consider imposing liquidated
damages or terminating the contract with the contractor for the
unimplemented construction of the perimeter fence and portion of the
pathway. Likewise, require the Municipal Engineer to review of the
engineering design if the perimeter fence is still feasible considering the
change in location and area/demographics.
Henceforth, ensure that all issues on the project site are settled before
the implementation of the project is started to avoid or mitigate
interruptions.

We recommended that the Local Finance Committee properly determine
the source of funds of any realignment before forwarding the
recommendation to the Sangguniang Bayan for enactment. Further, we
recommended that the MPDO monitor the implementation of the PPAs
procured and implemented by the LGU so as to ensure that the 20%
Development Fund shall only be appropriated and utilized for eligible
projects.

These audit recommendations are without prejudice to the issuance of
Notices of Suspension and/or Disallowance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the BAC to
submit all the necessary documentation prescribed in RA 9184 and COA
Circular 2009-001 within the prescribed period so as to timely
communicate any noted deficiencies and/or defalcations, and ensure to
strictly observe the procurement processes before issuance of the Notice of
Award.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough
review of all claims for honoraria of officers and employees involved in
government procurement so that it will be supported with complete
documentation before approval for payment pursuant to COA Circular
2012-001 or 2023-004. Further, ensure that the BAC members shall only
be granted with honoraria for those successfully completed procurement
activity.

We recommended for the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal
Officials concerned to coordinate and implement measures to improve the
storage environment including the inventory management system to
facilitate tracking, monitoring and replenishment of stocks.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor thru the Solid Waste
Management Board to monitor and evaluate the current controlled
dumpsite of the Municipality and scout for another location that will be
appropriately operated as landfill, if necessary.

We recommended that the Solid Waste Management Board to require all
barangays to establish an operational MRF. Likewise, conduct regular
inspection and monitoring of the established MRFs to ensure the
operationalization of these facilities including waste diversion and effective
record management. Further, enforce strict implementation of the
sanctions provided in the Municipal ordinances and RA 9003.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Treasurer to ensure collection of amounts from the persons liable for the
settlement of the disallowed transactions prescribed under COA
Circular No. 2009-06.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to immediately submit to the audit
team the required documents relative to the Notices of Suspension to avoid
maturing the NS into disallowance in audit pursuant to Section 82 of
Presidential Decree 1445.

1.9. We recommended and the Management agreed the implementation of
these courses of actions:

a. The Municipal Treasurer’s Office personnel in-charge of the maintenance
of the check register faithfully and diligently update the register and
include only those released checks in the Monthly Report of Check issued
for submission to the Accounting Office; and

b. The Municipal Accountant record the transaction only upon the receipt
of the RCI from the Treasury Office.

3.11. We recommended and the Management agreed to implement the following
courses of action:

a. The Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Engineer and
compute the appropriate liquidated damages for the delayed
completion of the project – Improvement of Drainage Canal; and

b. The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant cause the
immediate return of the unexpended balances to the Bureau of
Treasury in compliance with the budget circulars governing each
flagship program.

4.9. We recommended and Management agreed to include in the LDRRMF
Investment Plan during budgeting process, a separate caption for the list
of programs, projects and activities to be charged under the unexpended
LDRRMF of previous years in accordance with Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular
No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and to ensure that unexpended
LDRRM Fund will only be used on programs, projects and activities identified
in the LDRRMF Investment Plan.

5.8. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Treasurer agreed to implement the following courses of actions:

a. Install controls and strategies so that the prepared checks will be issued
promptly to its respective payees to avoid staling;

b. Strictly follow the proper transfer of financial documents from MTO to
MAO to avoid loss and misplaced documents; and

c. Be consistent in submitting complete DVs with its supporting documents
on time for the conduct of judicious audit and verification thereof.

6.10. We recommended and the Management agreed to implement the following
courses of actions:

a. Require the contractor to rectify the noted deficiency amounting to
₱4,758,119.10, otherwise, the same amount be disallowed in audit; and

b. Diligently conduct final inspection of all completed projects before
accepting to avoid payments resulting loss to the government.

7.11. We recommended and the Management agreed to implement the following
courses of actions:

a. The Municipal Engineering Office to conduct thorough assessment of the
condition of the building to determine the extent of damage and require
the contractor to do additional works for areas that need remedial
actions; and

b. Prioritize the allocation of funds for the establishment of access roads
that will lead to the immediate utilization and operation of these two
infrastructure projects.

8.8. We recommended and the Management agreed to implement the following
courses of actions:

a. Prioritize the allocation of funds for the installation of power supply at the
Residual Containment Area so that the waste shredder and composter
given by DENR-EMB will be operational;

b. The Municipal Health Officer communicate with the source agency for
the operating manual of the transferred infant radiant warmer for it to be
functional; and

c. The Environment and Natural Resources, and Health Offices secure
copies of the Property Transfer Report and/or Deed of Donation from the
source agencies to aid the Accounting Office in booking up these
equipment in the books of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following:
 
the Municipal Engineer to strictly adhere to the provisions and guidelines in the preparation of the ABC and perform a detailed review of the Program of Works to ensure that the basis and ceiling of bid prices are acceptable/reasonable and advantageous to the government; and

inform the contractors to refund the amount of ₱31,435.53.



We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor directed the TWG to conduct an in-depth post-qualification considering the accuracy of the computation of detailed estimates, specifically on the OCM mark-up.
observations.managementAction:
