observations.managementAction: The GSO had submitted RPCPPE in RCPPE in prescribed format prescribed in Section 7 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II and to prepare the same inventory properly and not as mere subsidiary records of PPE accounts; The GSO provide details of the property totaling P274,861,790.44 taken up in the RPCPPE in lump sum; the General Services Officer submit documents relating to the donated and disposed PPEs to the Accounting Office for recording/dropping from the books; and the GSO undertake proper disposal of unserviceable property.
observations.reason: Difficulty in retrieving old records hindered the immediate reconciliation of both records.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Services Office to:
a) submit the complete RIS and SSMI pertaining to the issuance of 500 sets of hygiene kits to the Audit Team and thereafter submit the same to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for proper recording in the books; and
b) conduct a complete physical count of all inventories and submit the corresponding Report of the Physical Count of Inventories to the Office of the Auditor within the period prescribed under Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The agency had ongoing. Partially Implemented Reports are to be submitted to the Auditor.
observations.reason: Physical inventory report was not yet submitted to the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to continue issuing demand letters to all concerned officers and employees to settle their outstanding cash advances immediately, otherwise, cause the withholding or suspension of their salaries for non-compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Municipal Development Council and approval of the Sangguniang Bayan re-appropriate the above-cited prior years implemented projects to other priority development projects to provide optimum benefits to the public or intended beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: The municipality implemented prior years projects to other priority development projects to provide optimum benefits to the public or intended beneficiaries.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the concerned Municipal officials:
1. prepare and issue policies and guidelines to address the issue on the necessity and propriety of hiring JOs;
2. incorporate in the JO contract the number of man hours, quantity and duration of tenure of contract, the works to be performed by the job order personnel and require them to submit daily accomplishment reports duly quantified; and
3. in coordination with the department heads, assess and review the necessity of hiring job orders by considering the existence of regular employees who could perform some of the contracted work. Maximize the services of the regular employees and minimize the hiring job orders.
observations.managementAction: Review of the number of JOs ongoing. JO Contracts had been revised. Meetings and coordination with the department heads had been made.
observations.reason: Preparation of policies and guidelines was ongoing. The GSO and departments were undertaking coordination and revisions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor effect the adoption of the automated payroll system to address the risk of loss and/or misappropriation of funds and to ensure better control over handling of cash and administrative efficiency.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality adopted the automated payroll system in November 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council to prepare a comprehensive MDRRMFIP with specific/detailed date of implementation of all PPAs for both current and continuing appropriations and instruct the MDRRMO to prepare and submit to the Audit Team the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of MDRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMIP was ongoing.
observations.reason: The Municipal Disaster Risk Management Officer was still unable to submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of MDRRMF on or before the 15th day.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to consolidate the different offices/departments PPMPs prepared by the different offices/ departments and integrate them into one APP for proper approval and use as basis of procurement in bulk in accordance with the intent and requirements of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR. We further recommended the Municipality use public bidding as the primary mode of procurement and discontinue procuring goods through shopping unless the condition in Section 52.1 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 is met.
observations.managementAction: PPMPs prepared by the different offices/departments were being consolidated into one APP. The Municipality used public bidding as the primary mode of procurement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to require the submission of an irrevocable standby Letter of Credit, bank guarantee, or surety bond callable upon demand issued by a surety or insurance company duly licensed by the Insurance Commission and confirmed by the agency, contrary to Section 4.2, Annex E of the RIRR of RA 9184, to adequately protect the Municipality in case of default or non-compliance to the terms of the Contract by the contractor.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had required the submission of all supporting documents prior to the payment to the concerned contractor and henceforth, see to it that claims for mobilization/advance payment are supported with all the required documents prior to the payment thereof.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD Focal Point System, include representatives from private sector, Women’s Organization and recognized/accredited non-government organization as members of GFPS to ensure that gender issues from these sectors will be raised and addressed.
observations.managementAction: The agency had coordinated with private sector and women's organization for inclusion in the GFPS.
observations.reason: Awaiting the names of the nominees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management monitors the outstanding balance of their receivables and exert more effort on collecting those money owed to the borrowers.
observations.managementAction: Management send demand letters to the borrowers.
observations.reason: has difficulty locating the whereabouts of other Government.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the completed projects to its appropriate accounts and adjust the accounts affected for fair presentation of the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: Reclassification of the CIP accounts to its proper billing, though projects were already partially completed, thus adjusting the CIP account to its appropriate PPE accounts could not be undertaken.
observations.reason: Contractors have not yet claimed the 100% billing; projects were already partially completed, thus adjusting the CIP account to its appropriate PPE accounts could not be undertaken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management undertakes extensive planning related to disaster risk reduction and management to preclude the existence of same conditions mentioned above and to avoid unfavorable observation in the future.
observations.managementAction: Management refrained from charging the ineligible expenditures to LDRRM and undertakes planning related to disaster risk reduction and management.
observations.reason: Due to lack of funds allocated for the operation of the LDRRM Office, maintenance of the office were still charged to the fund but very minimal in percentage now.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that Management strictly adheres to the legislative intent of RA 9184 especially on the participation of its employees’ cooperatives in their own procurement process to preclude unfavorable observations in the ensuing years.
We recommended that management will be mindful of the provisions of RA 9184 and its intentions to preclude same unfavorable observations in the future.
We recommended that Management closely monitor the project implementation to ensure completion of projects on time, thus enhancing disaster prevention, preparedness and response.
observations.managementAction: Management closely monitor the project implementation to ensure completion of projects on time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly monitors the programmed PPAs of GAD so intended beneficiaries could avail of the public services that addresses gender issues and concerns.
We recommended that Management utilize fully on a timely basis any downloaded funds for the immediate enjoyment of benefit derived therefrom.

We recommended that Municipal Planning Officer strictly monitors the implementation of the project and make sure projects are implemented in accordance with the target date so as not to deprive the end users of its beneficial use.

We recommended that the Local School Board strictly monitors the implementation of the project and make sure projects are implemented in accordance with the target date so as not to deprive the students of their immediate use and the benefits derived therefrom.
observations.managementAction: Management has fast tracked its completion.
observations.reason: Delayed completion was due to unforeseen problems that arose.

observations.recommendation:
1.9 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant
to properly prepare a Bank Reconciliation Statements for all the bank accounts
maintained by the Municipality, and immediately prepare the necessary adjusting
entries in the books for the reconciling items.
1.10 We also recommended for the submission of the copies of Bank Statements to
facilitate the verification of Bank Reconciliation Statements.

2.12 We recommended the Local Chief Executive to instruct:
a) The officers and employees concerned for the immediate settlement of their
outstanding cash advances;
b) The withholding of the salaries of the accountable officers until they make
liquidation of their cash advances;
c) The Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to the employees
concerned and forward the same to COA for the issuance of final demand,
and initiate the filing of cases pursuant to Section 128 of PD 1445; and
d) The Municipal Accountant to stop the grant of additional cash advance
unless the previous cash advance given to the officers and employees is first
settled or a proper accounting thereof is made, and issue a certification that
the employee has no outstanding cash advance for every cash advance
granted.

3.11 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer furnish the Municipal Accountant
with a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible for the year based on the Real Property Tax Account Register.
3.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal
Treasurer and Municipal Assessor to establish the correct amount of receivables and
draw adjusting entries to correct the balances of the accounts.

4.13 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Treasurer/General Services
Officer, and Engineer to comply with the Accounting and Reporting Procedures on
Local Road Asset Management System, to perform their mandated duties and
responsibilities in the submission of reports and maintenance of records, and to
coordinate with each other.
4.14 Submit copies of the Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE), and Property,
Plant and Equipment Ledger Card (PPELC)
4.15 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw Journal Entry Vouchers
to correct the errors in the books of the LGU relative to the computation of
depreciation.

5.8 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant shall reconcile the balances of the
reciprocal accounts of different funds and record the necessary adjustments to bring
their balances into agreement.
5.9 We also recommended that he shall regularly monitor the transactions affecting
reciprocal accounts and ensure that these are recorded in the different funds
simultaneously to avoid any error of omission, and settle immediately the intra-
agency transactions to ensure regular updates and to avoid erroneous account
balances.

6.9 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant-
Designate, Municipal Treasurer, and all responsible officials to immediately submit
the disbursement vouchers, together with its supporting documents, and cancelled
checks. This is without prejudice to any notice of suspension, charges or
disallowances that may be issued as a result of our audit.
6.10 Ensure that all disbursement vouchers, pertinent supporting documents and copies
of the checks be submitted on a regular and timely basis.

7.10 We recommended to require all the personnel concerned, especially the municipal
accountant, to attach complete documentations on all disbursements, to ensure the
validity as well as the correctness of the transactions. Significantly, management
should strictly adhere to the policy of no payment without complete documentation.

8.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer be
required to remit in full the taxes withheld on compensation and payments to
contractors and suppliers on or before the due dates.

9.13 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant
and the Municipal Treasurer to observe the due dates of the remittances of premium
and loan amortization to Pag-IBIG, PhilHealth and GSIS.

10.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
a) The Municipal Budget Officer and all those officials involved in the
preparation of the Annual Budget to properly observe the guidelines, and
prepare the LGU budget in accordance with the Budget Manual to prevent
delays in the approval of the same by oversight bodies;
b) The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC),
Municipal Engineer, and other implementors to coordinate with each
other to ensure the full implementation of the 20% DF PPAs during the
budget year.

11.6 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive shall require the PESO
Manager and all other concerned in the implementation of the TUPAD Workers
Program to determine the causes of the delay. Henceforth, ensure that the
stipulations in the MOA are faithfully carried out to achieve the purpose of the
program on a timely basis.

12.10 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal
Planning and Development Coordinator, and all other concerned shall ensure that
all PPAs to be funded by the 20% DF are eligible and implemented during the
budget year to attain optimum utilization of the fund and enjoyment of benefits by
the populace.
12.11 We also recommended that all concerned officials, who are responsible for the
preparation of the Municipal Budget shall carefully observe the provisions of DILG-
DBM JMC No. 2017-1 to prevent inclusion of ineligible PPAs.

13.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall require all the personnel
concerned, especially the Municipal Accountant, to attach complete documentations
on claims, to ensure the validity and propriety of the transactions. Henceforth, shall
not process transactions, which are deficient in supporting documents.

14.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the identified Persons
Responsible and Liable in the Notices of Suspension and Notices of Disallowance,
respectively to settle their audit suspension or disallowance.
14.10 We recommended further that the Municipal Accountant send copy of the
Notices to the Persons Responsible/Liable for their information on their unsettled
suspensions/disallowance.

15.9 To correct the deficiencies, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Budget
Officer, and the Municipal Accountant:
a. Furnish the audit team copies of the Job Order Contracts/Service
Contracts within five days after its approval. Ensure that it states the
expected output/service to be rendered, duration of work, office of
assignment, source of funds and its daily rate; and
b. Require each JO worker to prepare the accomplishment report, stating
therein the actual and specific tasks undertaken during the period, duly
verified and signed by the immediate supervisor, and attached to the
Payroll with the Daily Time Record.

16.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person and
Municipal Budget Officer to strictly comply with the provisions of PCW-DILG-
DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 on prioritization, and training of personnel
responsible for planning and implementation of GAD PPAs to ensure that they are
aligned with GAD-related undertakings, and emerging and/or continuing gender
issues and concerns, so that the intended beneficiaries are assured of the services,
protection, and other assistance that may be provided.
16.12 We also recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan pass a local GAD code or
ordinance with the assistance of the LGU GFPS, to support the localization of
Magna Carta of Women and to ensure that GAD is mainstreamed in local policy-
making and integrated in local development plans.

17.10 We recommended that the LCE and the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Council procure the basic equipage prescribed by DILG to ensure that
the municipality is prepared for calamities as prescribed in NDRRMC, DBM and
DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1.

18.11 We recommended that the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board, which is
chaired by the Local Chief Executive, should annually update the LGU’s MSWMP.
18.12 We also recommended that management should strictly observe the provisions of
RA 9003, especially in complying with the minimum standards and requirements
for the collection of solid waste, and the establishment of a Sanitary Landfill.

We recommended that management direct the Local Finance Committee to monitor and evaluate the physical and financial implementation of the projects so programmed projects will be implemented within the scheduled time frame and for the constituents to enjoy the beneficial use of it. Further, that management direct the contractor/supplier to refund the excess payments of the above mentioned projects with over claims as well as the service provider of the claim for Php24,000.00 since the project was under contract by a certain construction firm.
observations.managementAction: The management did not act on it.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management give preferential attention to this concern by prioritizing the budget allocation for the final waste disposal/processing facility to prevent the incurrence of possible health hazards the existing dumpsite might cause to the constituents.
observations.managementAction: The management did not fully implement the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management undertake extensive planning related to disaster risk reduction and management to preclude the existence of the same conditions mentioned above and to avoid unfavorable observation in the future.
observations.managementAction: The management did not act on the recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Inventory Committee to conduct complete inventory of all the properties of the Municipality including public infrastructures;
Inventory Committee upon completion of the inventory, prepare and submit the required reports such as Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE), Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System; and
Municipal Accountant and GSO reconcile their records with the result of the inventory taking and investigate the differences.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to submit immediately the disbursement vouchers and deposit slips of the transactions presented as reconciling items; and
Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records and make the necessary adjustments in their records as needed.
Thereafter, reconciliation of records and reconciliation statements be regularly done and use as a monitoring tool and detective control.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause or order the Municipal Accountant to further look into previous years’ files in order to account for the said receivables.  If after all the possible means were exerted and the claims could not be accounted, request for write-off of the above receivables supported by above cited documents.

We have recommended that the Management strictly comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting and liquidation of cash advances.
In addition, we have recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Require those officials and employees with outstanding cash advances the purpose of which had been accomplished/served to immediately settle their accounts by submitting the required liquidation reports to the accounting office;
Require those officials and employees whose cash advances were not used for period of two months to return/refund immediately;  and
Institute legal action against those who still did not comply after due notice.

We have recommended that Management submit their legal basis in providing medical allowances and gift certificates to all elective officials, permanent and casual employees, including contract of service, otherwise the said payments will be disallowed in audit.
Thereafter, Management exercise prudence in the granting of incentives to its officials and employees and strictly adhere to the existing laws, rules and regulations to avoid disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to submit the application for leave of all employees and officials who availed the 10 days monetization.
Further, Management strictly comply with the documentary requirements in the payment of monetization of leave credits in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We have recommended that the concerned officials and employees refund the excess claims made against the Municipality.
Thereafter, we have recommended that Management use their available motor vehicles in their official travels.  In cases there are no available vehicles, to allow reimbursement limited to equivalent cost of the ordinary mode of transportation.
We further recommended that the Management exercise prudence in the utilization of resources to avoid wastage of government funds.

We have recommended and the Management agreed to stop the practice of spending public funds on meals and snacks for entertainment or reception of staff and guests.  Public funds must be used prudently at all times ensuring economy and propriety.
We further recommended that the BAC ensure that prices availed by the Municipality should be just and commensurate with the menu being served.

We have recommended that Management formulate guidelines for the conduct of its Team Building Activities, using as reference CSC Resolution No. 101262 and COA Resolution No. 2012-014 in order to promote economy and have a smooth, orderly, more effective and gender-responsive team building activity.  If possible, limit the allocation to P1,200.00 per participant.  Further, relative to COA Circular No. 2012-014, a report should be prepared by the Human Resource Department (HRD) for the proper evaluation of the TBA.

We have recommended that Management submit justification and legal basis for paying the above-mentioned transactions.  We further recommended that Management stop the practice of providing tokens and cash for birthday celebrants and cellphones to all employees and officials of the LGU.
Thereafter, the Municipal officials and employees ensure efficient and economical use of government funds to avoid wastage of government funds.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the drivers concerned to accomplish properly the Driver’s Trip Tickets to comply with the afore-cited regulation for proper control and monitoring of fuel expenses.
Further, for Management to adopt a sound internal control measures, procedures and guidelines in the use of fuel to avoid misuse and wastage of government resources.
We have recommended that the Municipal Engineer require the contractor to submit the results of material tests for the above-mentioned projects.

We have recommended and the Management agreed that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the Technical Working Group (TWG) strictly validate the technical requirements submitted by bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid in order to assess the competence and sufficiency of manpower and equipment pledged to each contract to ensure the timely and best quality of work for all the projects of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to submit suspension and resumption orders of the above-mentioned infrastructure projects together with PAG-ASA Weather Reports.
We have further recommended that in the absence of such documents, the City Engineer be directed to compute Liquidated Damages and require the contractor to pay the same to the Municipal Government.
Thereafter, suspension and resumption orders be provided with the Audit Team within five days upon its perfection.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to assess the causes of delays in the Programs/Activities/Projects (PAPs) implementation and address all issues accordingly in order to fast track the implementation of priority development PAPs under the 20% Development Fund in conformity with JMC No. 2011-1 of the DILG and DBM so that its constituents will enjoy the expected benefits/outcomes.

We have recommended and the Management agreed to implement the LDRRM Plan and utilize its budget accordingly.  Further, the LDRRMC review and revisit the unutilized prior years’ QRF and DRRMF-MOOE in order to maximize the benefits of the fund.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive (LCE) being the Chair of GAD Focal Point to assess the causes of delays in the Programs/Activities/Projects implementation and address all issues accordingly in order to fast track the implementation of priority PAPs embodied in GAD Plan and Budget in conformity with RA 9710 so the gender issues will be properly addressed and that the intended beneficiaries will enjoy the benefits thereof.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to submit the above-mentioned report not later than the 5th day of the ensuing month for the Audit Team to verify the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms submitted by the Accountable Officers.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal Treasurer to advertise through posting in municipal and market premises the vacant stalls so as to maximize the income from stall rental.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
• Create an Inventory Committee, who shall conduct an actual and complete
physical inventory of all Municipal properties;
• Instruct he General Services Officer (GSO) to prepare a complete Report on the
Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment.; and
• Instruct the GSO to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant for the
reconciliation of records between their two Offices to come up with an accurate
and fairly presented reconciled balances of PPE accounts in the financial
statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Acting Municipal Treasurer to send written notice to the payees of the
existence of the stale checks for replacement, if applicable, and comply with
the procedures in the handling of stale/spoiled checks as stated in Section 59
of the NGAS Manual Volume III; and
b. Municipal Accountant effect necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct
balance of the Cash in Bank – Local Currency Current Account.
Management’s Comment:
The Acting Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant have committed that they will
comply with the audit recommendations.
• Reconciling items not taken up in the in the General Ledgers/Financial Statements
amounting to ₱8,057,395.00
COA Circular 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 states the following:
“Section 3.1 –The depository/servicing banks shall furnish the Local
Accountant with the Bank Statement (BS) including the debit/credit
memos (DM/CM), paid checks, et., within five (5) days after the end of
each month which shall be the bases in the preparation of the monthly
Bank Reconciliation Statement;
Section 3.2 – The Local Accountants shall within ten (10) days from
receipt of the Bank Statement, reconcile the same (BS) with the General
Ledger (GL) and prepare BRS in five (5) copies;
Section 3.3 – The Accountant shall draw journal vouchers to record all
valid reconciling items that require adjustment and correction of the GL;
Section 3.4 – The duplicate and quadruplicate copies of the BRS
including paid checks, original copies of debit/credit memos, shall be
submitted to the Auditor concerned for verification within ten (10) days
upon receipt after the end of each month.”
Bank Reconciliation Statement (BRS) is a report which compares the bank balance as
per agency's accounting records with the balance stated in the bank statement
to ascertain the differences between the two, and to book changes to the accounting
records as appropriate.
Verification of the amounts presented on the submitted BRS for the six current accounts
of the municipality from LBP disclosed reconciling items totaling ₱8,057,395.00 (Annex
E). According to the Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer these
differences are attributed to interests earned and Credit Memos not yet recorded by the
Accountant; Debit Memos for BIR Electronic Filing Payment System (EFPS) and Payroll
of Municipal officials and employees; withholding taxes of interests earned; purchase of
check booklets; erroneous posting by bank and erroneous use of check.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect in the books the
necessary adjustments to record all the reconciling items noted duly supported
with valid documents to reflect the correct cash balance in the financial
statements and coordinate with the depository bank for the reconciliation of other
reconciling items on the bank balance. We further recommend that the Municipal
Accountant exert additional effort to regularly reconcile the balances for the
timely detection of variances, if any.
Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Accountant promised to immediately effect the necessary adjustments
noted and will coordinate with Landbank for all reconciling items.
• Variance of ₱2,372,872.55 between the Cashbooks and the General ledgers
Section 74 of PD No. 1445, otherwise known as the Government Auditing Code of the
Philippines provides:
“At the close of each month, depositories shall report to the agency
head, in such form that he may direct, the condition of the agency
account standing on their books. The head of the agency shall see to it
that a reconciliation is made between the balance shown in the reports
and the balance found in the books of the agency.”
Section 6.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 provides that the
accountable officer and the Accountant shall reconcile their records at least quarterly.
It is necessary that the record of the treasury and the accounting be reconciled to ensure
the correctness and accuracy of Cash in Bank account.
Moreover, Section 27, Chapter I of NGAS Manual, Volume II states:
“The cashbook shall be maintained by bank account and by fund and
shall be updated and balanced daily. The Accountable Officer shall, at
the end of the month or when required to do so by proper competent
authority, rule and foot the cashbook. x x x”
The Financial Statements disclosed the following balances of Cash in Bank account:
Cash In Bank General Fund SEF Trust Fund Total
Local Currency-
₱201,060,282.67 ₱6,595,978.28 ₱40,894,212.57 ₱248,550,473.52
Confirmation of bank account balances as of December 31, 2018 made with the
depository banks of the Municipality showed the following balances per account:
Account Number Amount
Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP)
2062-1000-55 ₱200,312,036.40
2062-1009-34 7,358,918.29
2062-1000-71 18,393,133.98
2062-1024-81 15,750,533.75
2062-1025-46 4,637,355.44
2062-1026-00 3,109,168.42
Total Cash in Bank per Confirmation ₱249,561,146.28
Comparison of the Cash in Bank account per General Ledger (GL) and Cashbook as of
December 31, 2018 showed the following balances/variances:
General Fund SEF Trust Fund Total
GL Balance ₱201,060,282.67 ₱6,595,978.28 ₱40,894,212.57 ₱248,550,473.52
Cashbook Bal. 202,641,793.22 6,595,978.28 41,685,574.57 250,923,346.07
Variance ₱ (1,581,510.55) ₱ 0.00 ₱ (791,362.00) ₱ (2,372,872.55)
It can be gleaned from the table above that the balance between the cashbooks and the
general ledgers under Special Education Fund was fully reconciled, however there were
variances of P1,581,510.55 and ₱791,362.52 under the General Fund and Trust Fund,
respectively. Inquiry with the Municipal Treasurer disclosed that the differences could
be attributed to the accumulated interest earned from the Electronic Filing Payment
System of the municipality enrolled with the LBP and other items enumerated in the
previous discussions. The Municipal Accountant readily admitted that she neglected to
record the same and will immediately prepare Journal Entry Vouchers to rectify all the
items requiring adjustment.
The existence of the variance, between the general ledger and the cashbook of the
General and Trust Funds renders the Cash in Bank accounts unreliable and inaccurate
thus, affecting the fair presentation of the financial statements.
We recommended that both Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant
reconcile, prepare and take-up the reconciling items noted to reflect the correct
balances of the Cashbook and Cash in Bank – Local Currency Current account.
Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant both assured the Audit Team
that they will coordinate with one another to reconcile and take-up all reconciling items
between the Cashbook and General Ledger balances of Bank– Local Currency Current
account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
i. Municipal Accountant -
a. reconcile the difference between the balances of Real Property Tax (RPT)
Receivable and Special Education Tax (SET) Receivable;
b. Conduct a regular reconciliation of records with the Acting Municipal Treasurer
pertaining to the set-up RPT and SET Receivables and Deferred Income Tax at the
beginning of the period and the consistent adjustment of these accounts upon
collection;
c. maintain the necessary subsidiary ledgers for the RPT and SET Receivables in
order to validate the balances of the accounts presented in the financial statements
and to ensure accuracy and reliability of the balances reported monthly; and
d. review all entries reflected in the general journal so as to ensure that all recorded
adjustments are correct and complete. Effect the necessary adjustment/s, if any.
ii. Acting Municipal Treasurer -
a. Conduct reconciliation of records with the Assessor’s Office in order to have a
uniform and updated taxpayers’ records for more reliable RPT/SET collectibles; and
b. Monitor payment of real property tax due for the current year and exhaust all efforts
and devise strategies to collect all delinquent real property taxes and encourage the
tax payer to pay their dues on time. Conduct verification on those outstanding
balances to determine the existence and completeness of the receivables and its
collectability.
iii. Municipal Assessor to expedite the updating/cleansing of their records for the
uniform and accurate reporting of the real properties to be assessed. Thereafter,
submit the Monthly Report of Assessment of Real Properties and Individual Tax
Payers Assessment to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office for reconciliation of their
individual records.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and her staff to continuously coordinate with the Provincial Accountant
and reconcile their records on receivables/payables and their corresponding
payables/ receivables in the books of the Province to establish the correct balances
of the reciprocal accounts in the corresponding subsidiary ledgers and to ascertain
the legality and validity of both claims so both parties may make the necessary
adjustments on their respective books of accounts, if warranted.
Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Accountant has committed for the continuous coordination with the Office of
the Provincial Accountant for the reconciliation of the Due from and Due to LGUs accounts
and will prepare the necessary JEV.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Praise Committee to submit
documents that will support their grant of the questioned incentive. Otherwise,
employees will be required to refund the total amount of ₱4,710,000.00.
For future grants of incentives, through PRAISE, we recommended that the
Municipal Mayor and Praise Committee:
• comply strictly with the policy, rules and regulations on the Civil Service
Commission’s Program on Awards and Incentives for Service Excellence
pursuant to CSC Memorandum Circular No. 01 s 2001.
• ensure that the rules, specific policies and guidelines in performance planning,
monitoring and evaluation and mechanism for recognizing the awardees are in
place before granting awards to the employees.
• consider individual appraisal or evaluation report assessing the performance the
employees by means of the agency’s established system of incentives and
awards.
• Ensure that monetary awards were given only when the accomplishments and
other personal efforts resulted in monetary savings and it does not exceed 20%
of the savings generated
Management’s Comments:
The Municipal Human Resource and Management Officer informed that the LGU officials and
employees have not received Performance Enhancement Incentive (PEI) since 2016,
hence, they granted under PRAISE in CY last 2018.
Since Rosario is one of the fast growing and improving municipality through the collaborative
effort of municipal officials and employees, she added that in November,2018, the PRAISE
Committee has convened and discussed matters on how to compensate the exemplary
effort contributed by each work force for meeting the vision, mission and objectives of the
municipality.
It was so fortunate likewise that several offices and employees of the Municipal Government of
Rosario had been conferred with several recognitions, citations and awards from the local,
provincial and national agencies.
Furthermore, the PRAISE Committee has seen to it:
• That, one of the thrust of this municipality is to encourage creativity, innovativeness,
efficiency, integrity and productivity in the public service by recognizing and
rewarding officials and employees, individually or in groups and employees are
cooperative enough and have contributed their utmost effort for the efficiency,
economy or other improvement in government operations or for extra ordinary acts
of services in the public interest hence, we grant the incentives.
• That, they are very much assured that the grant of PRAISE incentives has adhered to
the principle of providing incentives and awards based on performance, innovative
ideas and exemplary behavior of employees, hence they were given a citation and
awards.
• That, the grant of incentives to officials and employees was provided both monetary
and non-monetary awards and incentives to recognize and acknowledge the
contributed efforts of all employees and officials.
• That, the praise committee shall ensure that productivity, innovative ideas,
suggestions and exemplary behavior can be identified, considered, managed and
implemented on a continuing basis to cover employees at all level.
• That, the praise committee shall be responsible for the development,
administration, monitoring and evaluation of the awards and incentives system of
the agency.
Since one of the four (4) core functions of the Human Resource Management is rewards and
recognition, the PRAISE committee have come up with the decision of granting incentives to
officials and employees. What they did is giving a certain amount to each citations and awards
and then divide equally to all employees for the benefit of all.
Now, considering that the incentive was already given to officials and employees, we are
respectfully and humbly requesting your good office not to refund the amount and we promise
to complete the necessary documents needed and will abide with the rules and regulations set
forth by the CSC on granting of praise. We will promise and commit not to do the same again
in our granting of incentives for the future.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
The Audit Team is on the belief that the Municipality of Rosario failed to provide sufficient
documents to prove that the criteria in the selection of the awardees was met or within the
purview of the context of the revised policies on Program on Awards and Incentives for
Service Excellence (PRAISE) set by the Civil Service Commission Memorandum Circular
No. 1,2. 2001. Likewise, the disbursement of funds for this purpose were not supported with
any evaluation report of the nominees and the awardees prepared by the PRAISE Committee
that will validate specific exemplary behavior, innovative ideas, inventions, discoveries,
superior accomplishment, heroic deed and/or extraordinary acts or services and their
evaluation against established success indicators that should have been the basis of the
awards and incentive. Granting that the Municipality received various awards and
achievements, still there is no showing that those accomplishments, together with others,
resulted in monetary savings so as to qualify for monetary awards and that the latter does not
exceed 20% of the savings generated.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
• concerned contractors to rectify immediately the deficiencies noted to conform
with the approved program of works or forfeit the Performance Security and
consider blacklisting the concerned contractors who refuses to effect the
necessary corrections and submit documents to support the action taken;
• Inspectorate Team to conduct regular and strict monitoring and supervision to
ensure that all contractors undertake the projects in accordance with the
approved program of works; and
• LDRRM Officer to refrain from charging against 5% LDRRM Fund programs,
projects and activities which are not related to disaster risk management
activities, included, but not limited to the construction of evacuation centers
without the basic necessary amenities since these could be mere covered
courts/stage and the charging thereof could not be justified.
Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Engineer, Municipal Budget Officer, Local Risk Reduction Management
Officer, and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator submitted their reply
with the commitment that the necessary corrections on the deficiencies noted will be
addressed and recommendations will be observed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned officials the
establishment and implementation of specific guidelines and policies in the granting
of all kinds of financial assistance to its constituents which may provide, among
others, the following:
• Qualifications of the recipients, to include income levels which may be
determined by the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office (MSWDO)
for all kinds of financial assistance to individual and non-government organizations;
• Maximum and reasonable amount of financial assistance for each category;
• Frequency or maximum number of entitlements in a year;
• Mode of payment;
• Uniform authorization of documentary requirements such as Certificate of
Indigency, Social Worker Case study, Official Receipts and other basic
requirements to establish validity and correctness of the claims and
• Other conditions and limitations that the Municipality may impose.
Also, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop granting financial assistance
for private purposes and comply strictly with the provisions of RA No. 7160.
Management’s Comment:
Management’s comments are yet to be submitted by the concerned officials.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor direct the focal person to strictly adhere to
the set guidelines of the approved Project Brief and Specific Implementation
Agreement (SIA) in order to improve and sustain the nutritional status of the target
children beneficiaries and ultimately contribute to the success of the program as a
whole.
We also recommended that focal person explain why:
• the number of days of program implementation was cut short to 65 days only
instead of 120 days;
• the total budget for the program of P2,903,400.00 was fully utilized despite of a
shortfall of 55 days; and
• she failed to refund the unused funds amounting to P376,237.00 to DSWDO at
the end of program implementation.
Management’s Comments:
Management commented that considering the rice requirement of the program, the
standard is an average of 10 children sharing a kilo of rice. For the just concluded program,
the Rosario SFP used a higher standard for rice consumption of 8 children sharing a kilo
of rice. For the hot meal component, the menu prepared strived to maintain the equivalent
to 1/3 of the Daily Recommended Energy and Nutrient Intake (ENI) used in the preparation
of hot meals. The Municipal Nutrition Action Officer took the liberty to enhance the cycle
menu since nutritionally adequate food items in the community is available at a reasonable
cost. The budget for the food allocation, however was good only for 65 days.
With regards to the number of Day Care Centers (DCC), the former and now retired
MSWDO/Focal Person committed typographical error. The actual number of DCC is 52
excluding the Supervised Neighborhood Play (SNP). While the number of children
beneficiaries1 ,616 and the variance of 3 is attributable to the fact that 1,613 is only an
estimated figure for the purpose of Project Brief submission.
She also failed to submit the Masterlist of Beneficiaries and Nutrition Status Report due to
the fog of excitement over retirement concerns.
The OIC-MSWDO commented that the NFA rice that they have purchased amounting to
P376,237.00 were actually distributed to the recipient beneficiaries and were consumed by
them.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned Municipal Officials
to undertake careful planning and execution of the proposed annual budget on the
basis of realistic projection of revenues for the ensuing year, conduct proper review
of past collection performances and analysis of the adequacy of the proposed
revenue collection targets in accordance with the intent of local government
budgeting that will support the proper allocation of expenditures.
Likewise, we recommended that periodic review should be made by the concerned
officials in the Municipality to ascertain whether collection targets are attained. The
reasons for the shortfall in collections should be analyzed and alternative actions
should be looked into to avoid incurrence of misses in projections in the future.
Management’s Comments:
The Acting Municipal Accountant assured the Audit Team that she will coordinate with the
Local Finance Committee to employ conservative forecasting of expected income on the
next budget year; will make assessment of collection efficiency; and intensify activities to
meet targets. Coordination was made with the designated Business Permits and Licensing
Officer who informed her that a substantial number of motor cycle franchise holder did not
renew their franchise resulting to the decline of collection. Likewise, some establishments
failed to comply with the legal process of retiring their businesses resulting to their
inclusion in the active status in the database. As for the economic enterprise, 76 stalls under
the wet section of the municipal public market underwent renovation that lead to a
substantial reduction in collection.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MSWDO to prepare an
updated master list indicating the economic status of each senior citizen. The
MSWDO must ensure that all the eligible and qualified senior citizens are duly
selected and replaced taking into account the order of priority and economic
qualification provided under the DSWD Administrative Order No. 15 series of 2010
dated December 13, 2010. Finally, conduct quarterly validation and monitoring to
the beneficiaries to ascertain their entitlement and the propriety of the utilization of
the stipend for the full implementation of Social Pension Program of the government.
Management’s Comments:
The OIC-MSWDO and Focal Person commented that she has noted the Audit Team’s
recommendation to ensure the attainment of the program objectives. In addition, her Office
is now in the process of updating the Municipality’s Master List of Senior Citizens. Further,
the Department of Social Welfare and Development Office (DSWDO) Regional Office IV-
A sent five validators for the verification and validation of social pensioner and additional
beneficiaries of the Social Pension Program for deletion and inclusion purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
• in coordination with the HRMO to regularly reconcile employees’ records at
GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG to identify the affected employees on the
unremitted balances and immediately remit the amounts due to these agencies;
• identify the cause/s of the over remittance for some accounts and make the proper
adjustments; and
• remit the due GSIS loan amortizations pursuant to Section 14.2, Rule III of the
Implementing Rules and Regulations of Government Service Insurance Act of
1997 or R.A. No. 8291 to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary financial expenses
in the form of imposed penalties.
Management’s Comments:
The MHRMO commented that their office is responsible only for the preparation of payroll,
while the Office of the Municipal Accountant is in charge for the remittances of employees’
payment for loan amortizations and premium contributions with the GSIS. Over-deduction
for loan payment occurs because employees failed to inform their Office that they will renew
their loans prior to the preparation of payroll, resulting to over/under deduction.
With regards to Agency Remittance Advice (ARA), she stressed that their Office is very
prompt in submitting to GSIS particularly accession, separation, birth discrepancies,
changes in salary, changes in personal information and the like.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Office of the Human
Resource Management, Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer’s Office
to discontinue the practice of allowing deductions on monthly loan amortizations
from the personnel’s payroll, even if loans were from authorized government
institutions and these would reduce the employee’s monthly net take-home pay to
an amount lower than Five Thousand Pesos (₱5,000.00). Strictly comply with the
aforementioned GAA provision.
Management’s Comments:
The HRMO commented that all allowable deductions are automatically deducted regularly
from the salary of all Municipal officials and employees such as withholding taxes,
premiums and retirement insurance for GSIS, Philhealth and Pag-IBIG; settlement of
government claims against the employee; disallowances; and deposits and repayment of
loans owing to government lending institutions or associations organized and managed by
government employees upon written authorization to the disbursing officer.
Relative thereto, for human considerations, officials and employees were allowed to avails
loans to GSIS and other financial institutions due to their urgent financial needs.

We recommended for the following corrective measures:
• the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer exert more effort to
reconcile the difference of P117,538,536.86 existing between the accounting
records of PPE and RPCPPE;
• the General Services Office (GSO) submit RPCPPE in the format prescribed in
Section 57 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II and to prepare the same properly
and not as mere subsidiary records of PPE accounts;
• the GSO provide details of the property totaling P274,861,790.44 taken up in the
RPCPPE in lump sum;
• the General Services Officer submit documents relating to the donated and
disposed PPEs to the Accounting Office for recording/dropping from the books; and
• the GSO undertake proper disposal of unserviceable property.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Services Office to
conduct a complete physical count of all inventory items; prepare and submit the
corresponding Report of the Physical Count of Inventories to the Office of the
Auditor within the period prescribed by Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs,
Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
continue issuing demand letters to all concerned officers and employees to settle
their outstanding cash advances immediately, otherwise, cause the withholding or
suspension of their salaries for non-compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure adherence to the provisions of
Section 93 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002 and limit payments for the
purchases or other payment of claims through reimbursement to the amount not
exceeding P15,000.00. Otherwise, payment checks should be made directly to the
supplier’s net of taxes due the government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor in coordination with the Municipal
Development Council and approval of the Sangguniang Bayan re-appropriate the
above-cited savings from the implemented prior years projects to other priority
development projects to provide optimum benefits to the public or intended
beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor effect the adoption of the automated
payroll system to address the risk of loss and/or misappropriation of funds and to
ensure better control over handling of cash and administrative efficiency.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction Management Council to prepare a comprehensive MDRRMFIP with
specific/detailed date of implementation of all PPAs for both current and continuing
appropriations and instruct the MDRRMO to prepare and submit to the Audit Team
the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of MDRRMF on or before the 15th day
after the end of each month.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, being the head of the procuring entity
provide valid justification for resorting to shopping as the mode of procuring the
equipment enumerated above for evaluation by the Audit Team.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to consolidate the
PPMPs prepared and submitted by different offices/departments and integrate them into
one APP for proper approval and use it as basis of procurement in bulk in accordance
with the intent and requirements of RA No. 9184 and its RIRR.
We further recommended the Municipality use public bidding as the primary mode of
procurement and discontinue procuring goods through shopping unless the condition in
Section 52.1 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 is met.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
require the submission of an irrevocable standby Letter of Credit, bank guarantee,
or surety bond callable upon demand issued by a surety or insurance company
duly licensed by the Insurance Commission and confirmed by the Municipality for
the advanced amount to the concerned contractor and henceforth, see to it that
claims for mobilization/advance payment are supported with all the required
documents thereto prior to the payment thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD Focal Point System,
include representatives from private sector, Women’s Organization and
recognized/accredited non-government organization as members of GFPS to
ensure that gender issues from these sectors will be raised and address.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to instruct the Municipal
Accountant to henceforth prepare the Journal Entry Vouchers for every
transactions recorded in the books of accounts. Also, sternly remind the
Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the General Ledgers,
Subsidiary Ledgers and the Journals.

We recommended for the Local Chief Executive to instruct the Municipal
Accountant to verify the account to determine the correctness in the
charging to the account. It is further recommended that the Municipal
Accountant to be wary in the recording of transactions so as not to
misrepresent the accounts in the financial statements.
Also, the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant should
coordinate to prevent the incurrence of expenditures beyond the approved
budget.

We recommended that management strictly comply with the required
submission of contracts to the Audit Team within five (5) days from their
issuance or execution, for transactions to be reviewed as to legality and
propriety prior to their payment. Moreover, continued refusal to adhere
as required shall subject concerned officers to the legal consequences
provided for under the rule in case of non-compliance.

To address the issues, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive
appoint a DRRM Officer who shall work full time. In addition, assure
implementation of all approved PPAs under the DRRMF.

We recommended for the Local Chief Executive to:
c) Ensure that the appropriated 20% Development Fund should be
optimally utilized to achieve the desired socio-economic
development and environmental outcomes in the LGU; and
d) Ensure that only allowable development projects expressly
allowed in the DILG-DBM Circular No. 2017-1 dated February
22, 2017 should be charged under the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive prioritize funding the
implementation of the solid waste collection and disposal program of the
Municipality. Establish linkage with other government agencies to solicit
additional funds and involve the public in the program by creating
awareness of the procedures and benefits of proper waste disposal and to
motivate compliance.

Moreover, the program and its implementation should cover all the eleven
barangays of the municipality or else the program will be useless.

To address the issues, we recommended the following:
e) The Local Chief Executive (LCE) as a chairman and all other
members of the Local School Board (LSB) to formulate the three-
year program for SEF which shall be the basis for the utilization
of such fund;
f) The Local Chief Executive (LCE) should ensure that all public
schools within its jurisdiction should equitably share the SEF for
the benefit of all public students;
g) The Local Chief Executive (LCE) to optimally utilize the Special
Education Fund (SEF) for the welfare of its constituents; and
h) The Municipal Accountant/ Treasurer to ensure that
disbursement out of the Special Education Fund (SEF) must be
pursuant to the budget prepared by the Local School Board.

To address the issues and concerns, we recommended the following:
c) The Local Chief Executive must provide GAD fund equivalent to at
least five percent (5%) of the total appropriation pursuant to PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2012-01; and
d) The Local Chief Executive take necessary steps to implement the
identified GAD PPAs for the welfare of its constituents and
compliance to the PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2012-1.

To correct these deficiencies, we recommended that:
c) The LCE and other local officials concerned to take necessary steps
to implement the projects subject in the fund transfer; and
d) The Local Chief Executive to initiate the return the funds received
to the source agency instead of being dormant in the bank.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to fill out immediately the
vacancy on the mandatory position for LDRRMO of the LGU pursuant to
the provision of RA 10121 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 so that disaster risk reduction
management will be address in times of natural calamities.

We recommended that Municipality formulate an action plan to
implement the audit recommendations, and submit immediately the duly
accomplished AAPSI form to the audit team; copy furnished the DBM, the
House Committee on Appropriations and the Senate Committee on
Finance, as mandated by Section 96 of RA10717.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive order the Municipal
Accountant to remit all taxes withheld to the BIR regularly and promptly
to avoid the incurrence of penalties and surcharges. All taxes withheld
should be remitted within ten (10) days following the month the
withholding was made.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the completed projects to its appropriate accounts and adjust the accounts affected for fair presentation of the Financial Statements.

We recommended that  management  undertakes  extensive  planning related to disaster risk reduction and management to preclude the existence of same conditions mentioned above  and  to  avoid unfavorable observation in the future.

We recommend that Management strictly adheres  to  the  legislative intent of RA 9184 especially on the participation of its employees’ cooperatives  in  their  own  procurement   process   to   preclude unfavorable observations in the ensuing years.
We recommended that management will be mindful of the provisions of RA  9184  and  its  intentions  to  preclude  same  unfavorable observations in the future.
We recommended that Management closely monitor the project implementation to ensure completion of projects on time, thus enhancing disaster prevention, preparedness and response.
We recommended that Management  strictly  monitors  the  programmed PPAs of GAD so intended beneficiaries could  avail  of  the  public services that addresses gender issues and concerns.
We recommended that Management utilize fully on a timely basis any downloaded funds for the immediate enjoyment of benefits derived therefrom.

We recommended that the Municipal Planning  Officer  strictly monitors the implementation of the project and make  sure  projects are implemented in accordance with the target date  so  as not to deprive the end users of its beneficial use.

We recommended that the Local School Board strictly monitors the implementation  of  the  project  and  make   sure   projects   are implemented in accordance with the target date so as not to deprive the students of  their  immediate  use  and  the  benefits  derived therefrom.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that proper documentation, sound management and fiscal administration should be observed in order to attain efficiency in the use of government funds.

Since fiscal responsibility rests upon the Head of the Agency, we recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to render her accounts and submit the required financial transactions together with the supporting documents to the Accounting Division, and the Municipal Accountant to submit the accounts and reports to COA on or before the 10th day of the succeeding month. observations.recommendation: We recommend that management properly observe the requirements under COA Circular No. 92-389 in order to maintain a sound internal control in the utilization of government funds. observations.recommendation: We recommend that management publish all their procurements in the PhilGEPS website. Generally, public bidding should be used for all procurement and all alternative modes of procurement be resorted to only if the conditions which allows the use of such alternative methods are met pursuant to the Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act 9184. Moreover, stop the practice of procurement thru reimbursements.

We recommend that management strictly observe the submission of contracts/purchase orders within five (5) working days and furnish delivery documents within twenty-four (24) hours from acceptance of deliveries of goods and services, in line with COA Circulars No. 2009-001 and 002. Likewise, payment of the various purchases should be supported with complete documentation as mandated in COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 for propriety and legality.

We recommend that management limit the grant of overtime services only when extremely necessary, within its appropriations, and supported with proper documents in accordance with CSC-DBM Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2015 on the policies and guidelines on overtime services of government personnel, Section 4(1) of PD 1445 and Section 5.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 for its basic documentary requirements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant initiate  a  thorough review of the affected accounts to determine the cause/s of the abnormal balances and make proper adjustments/corrections. Further, the Municipal Accountant must exercise due diligent and care in the performance of her duties and responsibilities to ensure the non-recurrence of the abnormal balances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a) Management to strictly adhere to the provision of Section 20, Volume I of the NGAS Manual in order to ensure reliability of the balances of Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable accounts; b) the Municipal Accountant to correct receivables set up in prior years and to maintain subsidiary ledgers for current and prior years account to have a detailed record of the accounts for facility of verification.

The Municipal Accountant did not maintain subsidiary ledgers for current and prior years account to have a detailed record of the accounts for facility of verification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to design effective tax strategies to intensify collection of Real Property Taxes, not only current taxes but also the delinquent taxes in order to generate more income. Also, enforce the tax remedies provided for under Book II of the Local Government Code in the collection of taxes due to the government.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality still did not design the tax strategies to intensify collection of Real Property Taxes for the Current Year 2017 since there were still delinquent property taxes presented in the financial statements.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer are not enforcing the tax remedies provided for under Book II of the Local Government Code in the collection of taxes due to the government.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the BAC in coordination with the Municipal Engineer comply strictly with the requirements of semestral submission of the PMR pursuant to Section 122 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 and GPPB Circular no. 03-15.
observations.managementAction: The BAC were submitting to the Auditor’s  Office the Monthly/ Quarterly Report of Publicized projects but not the list of all on-going government projects/programs/activities and those that are to be implemented during the year, and information on the posting of project signboards/public notices within ten days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity, as well as the Monthly Report of Publicized projects, contrary to COA Circular No. 2013-004.
observations.reason: The Municipality did not strictly comply with the requirements of semestral submission of the PMR pursuant to Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 and GPPB Circular no. 03-15.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Government, through the Municipal Development Council: a) ensure the mandatory 20% Development Fund appropriated out of Internal Revenue Allotment be optimally utilized to help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes to implement prioritized programs and activities to help achieve the desirable outcomes on socio-economic development and environmental management pursuant to the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011; b) come up with a well-defined mechanism in identifying priority programs and projects in order to project a feasible and appropriate results and outputs that can be delivered efficiently and effectively to the public on time; c) revert the balances of implemented projects to General Fund for re-budgeting in the ensuing year.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2017, the Municipality did not optimally utilize the 20% Development Fund for CY 2017. The results of the implementation of the 20% Development fund for CY 2017 were not efficiently and effectively delivered to the public on time since there were various projects unimplemented. The balances of the implemented projects is kept on continuing appropriations and not transferred to General Fund for re-budgeting.
observations.reason: The Municipality did not optimally implement and various projects unimplemented. The management said that they just keep the balances of implemented projects in continuing appropriations and did not transfer to General Fund for re-budgeting.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Local School Board ensure that the Special Education Fund be utilized only on programs/projects or activities that are included among the list of priorities to be funded by SEF pursuant to the provision of Joint Circular No. 01 s 1998 of the Department of Budget and Management (DBM), Department of Education, Culture and Sports (DECS) and Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) in order to promote quality education in every public schools of the Municipality of Rosario.
observations.managementAction: The Special Education Fund were again utilized in 2017 for projects which are not priority according to the DBM, DECS and the DILG a Joint Circular issued on January 2017.
observations.reason: The Municipality again charged SEF for non-priority expenditures as observed in CY 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor reconstitute the membership of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB) to conform to the requirements of RA No. 9003 (Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000) and to optimize the involvement of civil society as envisioned in the law. Require the 26 barangays to re-establish and effectively maintain their materials recovery facility that will receive the mixed waste for final sorting, segregation, composting or recycling before transferring the residual wastes to  sanitary landfill. We also recommended strict implementation and monitoring of collection and segregation at barangay level.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor did not reconstitute the membership of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB) to conform to the requirements under Section 12 of RA No. 9003. As of December 31, 2017, there were only 34 barangays which have established MRFs and there were 14 barangays which have no established MRFs.
observations.reason: Lack of funds, unavailability of lot where the MRF will be positioned and lack of action on barangay officials.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Person to: a. submit GAD Plans and Budget to DILG for review and approval and GAD Accomplishment Report based on DILG-approved GPB; b. require all departments/offices to include in the GAD Plan and Budget all PPAs that would address identified gender issues; c. ensure all PPAs in the GPB are adequately funded and fully implemented and include only eligible expenses in GPB and AR; and d. submit justification/explanation why honorarium of Barangay Nutrition Scholars (BNS), Barangay Day Care Workers (BDCW) and Barangay Health Workers (BHW)-CMV were charged to GAD Funds.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person did not submit the GAD Plans and Budget 2017 to the DILG Regional Office for its review and approval. The Municipal management had included again GAD ineligible expenses in their GPB and AR. For CY 2017, the management had not established a GAD database as a basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation.
observations.reason: The GAD Focal Person didn't submit the GAD Plans and Budget 2017 to the DILG and budget 2018 to the DILG. The municipality included again ineligible GAD expenses.

observations.recommendation: We again recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all concerned municipal officials and employees to institute legal actions against responsible persons in the deposit of municipal funds to private banks. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the position of the subject unrecovered deposits which is included in the Other Receivables account to present fairly the financial position of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant said that as of December 31, 2017, there were no action taken by the Management  for this issue.
observations.reason: The said balances are old balances and the Administration headed by Late Felipe Marquez did not file the case to persons involved.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended to the management the following:
Conduct physical inventory of all existing properties of the municipality as required under Section 124 of NGAS Manual;
Submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the auditor’s office not later than January 31 of each year; and
Maintain property cards duly reconciled with the accounting records pursuant to Sections 119 and 120 of the same manual.

We have recommended and the management agreed to prepare the following journal entry to properly classify the accounts mentioned above for fair presentation of the financial statements, to wit:
Other Structures                                             171,000.00
Other Transportation Equipment                 997,800.00
Water System                                               1,957,868.50
Other Property, Plant and Equipment      2,905,807.48
Road Network                                            10,602,920.62
Buildings                                                       4,609,769.06
Government Equity                                         21,245,165.66

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal Accountant to monitor the grant and liquidations of cash advances and fund transfer to NGAs/LGUs.  For dormant accounts, the management should request for write–offs taking into consideration the guidelines setforth under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 in order to present fairly the accounts “Receivables” in the financial statements.

We have recommended and the management agreed to comply with the guidelines on the utilization of 20% Development Fund as provided under Department of Interior and Local Government-Department of Budget and Management Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017, in doing so, more developmental projects that would benefit the constituents would be implemented.

We have further recommended and the management agreed that the amount of P50,209.68 be reverted back to the 20% Development Fund and the expenditures of P50,209.68 be charged to the General Fund.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive that the Municipal Treasurer should make payments by check except for petty expenses to avoid misappropriation and mishandling of cash and to comply with R.A. 9184 on the procurement activities of the municipality for transparency.

We have recommended to Local Chief Executive to comply on the utilization of additional one percent Special Education Tax so that the fund will be optimally used for the purposes cited above.

We have further recommended that the amount be reverted back to the Special Education Fund and the amount of P313,160.72 be charged to the General Fund.

We have recommended and the management agreed to limit the practice of serving meals and snacks to various visitors of the municipality and observe prudence in utilizing government funds.  In doing so, additional funds could have been used for programs and projects to promote the great welfare of the constituents.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to comply with the regulations cited above so as not to affect the benefits accruing to the officials and employees of the municipality.  In doing so, penalties, if any, that may be imposed on the employer, will be avoided.  We further recommend that the Municipal Accountant look into the reasons why there were over-remittances.

We have recommended and the management agreed to refrain from designating JOC as collector, instead designate an accountable officer who is a holder of permanent position and should be adequately bonded as required under the above-cited regulation to ensure protection of government funds in case of misappropriation.

We have recommended and the management agreed to transfer to the General Fund the 2012 unutilized balance of LDRRMF amounting to P164,744.38 as mandated under the above regulation in doing so, additional funds could be used for social services after subsequent enactment by the local sanggunian.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to prioritize the establishment of GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation as required under the above regulation.

We have recommended and the management agreed to strictly comply with the above-cited provisions of R.A. 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations and COA Circular No. 2012-001.  In addition, the management is required to submit the lacking documents noted to ensure the legality and validity of the procurement of infrastructure projects.

We have recommended and the management agreed that programs, projects and activities that would be charged to the 1% Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA) be in accordance with the list provided under the above regulation so that funds would be optimally used for the development and protection benefits that should have been enjoyed by the children.
We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to implement the priority programs and projects which remained unimplemented inorder to uplift the lives of its constituents.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to oblige the Municipal Engineer ensure compliance with the quality design standards in the preparation of plans and specifications in the construction of FMR to promote road safety to all road users and to prolong the useful lives of the FMR.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal Treasurer to advertise through posting in municipal and market premises the vacant stalls so as to maximize the income from stall rental.
